An interesting idea which I had not considered. On reflection, I would have 
thought that the CIPR (China, India, Pakistan, Russia) economies you mention 
ALREADY have the US and Europe at a disadvantage, and these economies are 
not only competing, but winning... This conclusion is based on the consumer 
goods I see available for sale in shops here, which come from these 
economies, as opposed to consumer goods which come from the USA or Europe. I 
realise this is a narrow view, but nevertheless, it doesn't look like they 
have anything to fear when it comes to "competing"...

To achieve this end, they have enlisted
> millions of hapless drones to drone on about the end of the world and that 
> the only fix is for American school children to walk to school and 
> everyone switch to renewable resources (like whale oil).
>
:-)

> China, Russia, et al have insisted they be exempted from environmental 
> concerns and that the U.S. (for example) curtail its economic engine. If a 
> coal fired power plant is so vile, why doesn't China stop building them 
> (they are opening at least one coal-fired plant per week)?

I'm sure natural attrition will curtail this. Sooner or later they will run 
out of coal...
>
> The fix - if there is one - will depend on technology because this world 
> can't be run off  sunbeams. And technology wins.

I would agree that our best hope for the future lies in technology. However, 
I would get very uncomfortable were the US to lose it's technology lead, and 
there are indications that some of the nations which have been playing 
"catch up"are now trying to take that lead. Korean scientists doing genetic 
experiments (which may possibly be banned in the US) produced cats that glow 
under UV light last week. While there may (or may not...) be a need for 
glowing pussies, the point is that they have the technology and 
understanding to produce them.

Today it's a cat that glows, tomorrow it may be cold fusion, or star 
travel...

>In the past year, the U.S. has reduced its green-house gas emissions by 
>over 6% while Europe (for example) has increased their by almost 3%.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> to you."

Er... whatever happened to Coolidge...?

Pete.
```

In article <c984dece-921c-4175-8a04-09fb4d23ba69@l1g2000hsa.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

Evidently you're incapable of posting a link that would support your claim.

>> >> [snip]
>>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>are merely the peak of that cycle. 

Try again. The last two years have seen far *fewer* hurricanes than normal, 
not exactly the peak of the cycle.

>I am not aware of any evidence to
>support the view that the current crop of hurricanes (or their
>severity) is related to GW. Indeed there is evidence that there are
>statistically no more hurricanes nor any more severe hurricanes than
>would naturally occur at this point in the cycle.

That, however, hasn't stopped a lot of hysterics from blaming them on GW. 
Guess you weren't paying attention during the summer of 2005, or you would 
have heard them.

Then when 2006 had no major hurricanes, they blamed *that* on GW, too.
>
>However, warmer oceans would be expected to lead to more frequent and
>more severe hurricanes.

And yet, that isn't happening. Obviously something's amiss with the 
expectation.

>> >> >4. Global weather patterns are constantly changing. We simply do not
>> >> >have *precise measurements* of global tempratures even 100 years ago,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>to resolve the problem?

I would rather that we wait for evidence that:
a) there is any problem at all that needs to be resolved
b) we had anything to do with causing it
c) we are able to anything about it
before taking any action.
>
>No answer required. I have a friend who is happy to throw his rubbish
>over the fence rather than spend seconds putting it in the trash bin.
>You and he are very much alike.

Back to the ad hominem arguments, I see...

```
Alistair wrote:
> 
> No answer required. I have a friend who is happy to throw his rubbish
> over the fence rather than spend seconds putting it in the trash bin.

I despise littering.  However, I don't separate my garbage either. 
Someone mentioned it earlier, but technology has a way of compensating 
for humans' lack of desire to change their ways.  Already, our current 
"recycling" program doesn't demand separating paper from plastic from 
metal - because there is technology to separate them.  It's a small leap 
to run *all* trash through a sorter, that pulls that stuff out without 
humans having to have two trash cans.  (15 years ago, when I worked for 
a restaurant, we had this thing that we ran the food waste through as we 
dumped it into the trash - it caught metal and prevented silverware from 
being thrown away.)
```

HeyBub wrote:
> LX-i wrote:
>> Alistair wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> It was a corporal who eye-balled everything you scrapped off your tray. 

Heh - I don't think that would have gone over with the folks that worked 
at this restaurant.  They didn't even like it when the kitchen manager 
told them to do something - and he was the nicest guy in the world. 
Now, I could understand them not liking the general manager; he had 
little patience for slackers.  I never had any trouble with him, but 
then again, I like giving my employer a good value for his money.  :)

<anecdote>
Speaking of the kitchen manager - I even helped him move when he got a 
job.  His wife had seizures from time to time, so she couldn't drive.  I 
drove she, his son, and their puppy to a hotel in the city where they 
were moving to, then caught a cab to the bus station and took a bus back 
up.  (I learned that a 3-hour layover between 0300 and 0600 in Atlanta, 
GA is *not* my idea of a fun time!)  He was there to pick me up, then he 
drove the truck down, towing his car.  (Of course, I had also helped 
load that truck the day before.)
</anecdote>
```

> accurate instruments can be discounted.

To measure true average global tempratures precisely, you have to have
*A LOT* of precise instruments covering the whole area.

> 2. A large number of such instruments have been increasingly
> encroached upon by cities and their incumbent "heat bubbles". Didn't
> someone already address this issue in this thread by referring to a
> scientist who adjusted for encroachment and concluded that GW was
> greater than had been measured?

How do we know his "adjustments" are correct, unless (once again) by
experimental verification? Answer: we don't.

What I'm wondering is, what are you going to say in several years when
there's not a shred of doubt that global warming was a hoax?  After
all, many of the same people crying "global warming!" now were crying
"new ice age!" 20 years ago. Did I miss the new ice age? :-)

news:FdB6j.18852$_m.8317@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I mentioned the studies by John Cristy et al concerning long-term
temperature changes in the San Joaquin Valley; he adjusted the historical
readings since 1910 "... for any change that could have altered the
individual station records...".  My point was the one that Judson is making:
his "adjustments" were completely unjustifiable by any scientific standard.
And even so, he found that the temperature had increased by 3 degrees C (5.4
degress F) since 1910.

My bigger point that was, if persons in favour of the global warming
hypothesis are guilty of bad science, so are those on the other side.

And I'm still waiting for the list of glaciers and ice fields that are
expanding!!!!!

PL

On Dec 21, 2:43 pm, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> tlmfru wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.iceagenow.com/List_of_Expanding_Glaciers.htm

That may be 'a list' but it is nonsense. The Abel Tasman, for example
has been retreating at its face for the last 25 years. When I visited
there a few decades ago, the car park had been made at the face, now
the face is half a mile away from it.

I note that it concentrates of the 'head', the start of it up the
mountain.  Glaciers are rivers of ice, the snow up the mountain either
stays in place if it is cold enough or flows into the 'river' if it it
pushed by sufficient weight or warms up so that a lesser weight can
make it move.

The fact that the head has grown shows that the snow and ice at the
top of the mountain has warmed up. The retreating of the face shows
that the bottom of the mountain has warmed up.
```

On 7 Dec, 17:31, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> > Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> to follow Him? And if you don't think that, why don't you believe Him?

This is too easy. Why would a minor Midianite deity be concerned with
the works of man? Christ was a MAN of his times and was clearly
influenced by the religious theories of his (superstitious) time.
Hence his belief in a god, in heaven and hell (even the Catholic
church has doubts about whether purgatory and hell exists. If it
denies those places then it must also deny the existence of heaven).
The Bible (the Word of God in the eyes of some) was written by humans
and has been adulterated over the centuries (remember the one that
said 'Thou shalt sin'?) hence the existence of different variants of
the texts.
```

On 3 Dec, 18:13, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> SkippyPB wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> dramatically when the fog rolls in).

CO2 in increased concentrations (currently 377 parts per million) can
lead to an increase in productivity of botanical systems. However, it
also increases the acidity of rain and of the oceans, both leading to
biota reductions. A decline in concentrations of CO2 may lead to a
reduced botanic production on land due to reduced availability of CO2
but may increase oceanic production. Swings and roundabouts.

Water vapour is also a greenhouse gas contributing more to the
greenhouse effect than CO2 and is likely to increase as the oceans
warm up.
```

On 1 Dec, 18:46, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
> I have maintained from the beginning that we *don't know for certain* if global
> warming is happening,

Therefore, YOU can not be certain that it is not happening.

> and even if we did, we *don't know for certain* that
> humanity is causing it.

All data shows that the most significant increase in global CO2 and
temperature has occured with increasing human population and
particularly the INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION. Coincidence? I think not.

> I never said that either one was absolutely not happening.
> It is the global warming proponents who have no doubts, which in and of itself
> is alarming, considering the uncertainty of the evidence.

There is no uncertainty to the evidence nor to the conclusions that
the scientists have drawn from the evidence. Any uncertainty exists in
your mind. I rant on about your religious beliefs because I view that
those beliefs are significant contributing factors in your ostrich-
like approach to this issue.

> It is this absolute,
> unshakable, blind belief, when the evidence doesn't warrant it that distresses me
> most.

Ditto re your religious beliefs and your posture on global warming.

> Such attitudes are the antithesis of what the scientific attitude should be.
>
> And please don't rate on about belief in God. The situations are completely
> different.

Quite right. One is based on evidence and the drawing of conclusions
from said evidence and the other is based on blind ignorance in the
absence of any evidence.

> If I believe in God and there is no God, then I am no worse off;
> I will die and never know it. If you don't believe in God, and He does exist,
> you are in trouble that makes global warming look like a picnic, for eternity.

No trouble - THERE IS NO GOD.

>
> The situation with global warming is different. If you global warming advocates
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> we will know for certain at some point

We know for certain NOW!

> and will deal with it then.

How much evidence do you need before you will change your views? Do
you need the Simpson-esque hand of God to come down from the skys and
order you to change?

> If global
> tempratures have increased only one degree in a century, we aren't going to be
> fried in another 50 years, or even 100 years,

The current standpoint has it that if we do not start doing something
now then in 20 (and most certainly 50) years time it will be too late
to reverse the trends and prevent the forthcoming environmental
catastrophe. I believe that those who see global warming as a gentle
increase in temperatures over time are deluding themselves. I foresee
a tipping point which will have icecaps melted and temperatures
increased non-linearly; a viewpoint which is not ruled out from the
range of possibilities for the future.

As an aside, I presume you are happy to have the massive influx of
immigrants that will come to the Americas looking for land to live on
after their countries are turned into deserts?


> if it takes that to know.

And still the nay-sayers will cry "Falsehood!" We still have people
alive who believe the earth is flat, despite the evidence to the
contrary.

> d it
> would be expected that by then we would have better technology to deal with it
> if necessary.

The Republican viewpoint: "We don't need to do anything now because in
50 years we will have technology save us".

>If we blow ourselves up in a war in the meantime, it becomes moot.

Unlikely to happen.

>
> > As good a time as any. Except when it is too late.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -

In article <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
>All data shows that the most significant increase in global CO2 and
>temperature has occured with increasing human population and
>particularly the INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION. Coincidence? I think not.

Of course it's not a coincidence -- because it's not true.

There are NO reliable records of GLOBAL temperatures prior to the late 19th 
century, for three reasons: first, early thermometers were not accurate enough 
to allow valid comparisons with modern data; second, record-keeping was spotty 
and inconsistent; and third, temperature measurements were neither made nor 
recorded *globally*.
```

In article <edc2bf5a-c795-4202-92d0-2a4f46705c58@w40g2000hsb.googlegroups.com>, Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>On 2 Dec, 15:43, spamb...@milmac.com (Doug Miller) wrote:
>> In article
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>isotopes in marine snail shells.

You can *infer* temperature from those things, but you can't *measure* it. And 
the global-warming Chicken Littles would have us make massive changes in every 
facet of life on the basis of an inference. I'd rather have actual 
measurements, thank you very much.

I am sure of myself and , when TIMFRU invents a time machine, he can
go back in time to take the measurements for himself and prove the GW
brigade wrong. I won't hold my breath.

Judson, I realise that my belief in GW is as strong as your scepticism
but is founded in observable fact and not in ignorance or scepticism.
I do not think it is too late to save the world but I do think that,
without support from the entire population of the world, I will be
able to sunbath outside my flat in 2050 on christmas day with the
waves lapping around my feet on the beach (currently 200 feet above
sea level).

As for name calling? Calling you a worshipper of a minor Midianite
storm god is an accurate observation. Look up where Yahweh came from
on Wikipedia for yourself and you will see what I am getting at. By
the way, It was a novel by James Branch Cabell that appraised me of
that one.

isn't subject to controlled experiments.  I simply can't understand the

cap vanishing and the Antarctic ice fields breaking off the continent and
floating away, hundreds of square miles at a time.

withdrawing!

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5stp9cF1argf9U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <d5ork3l3ndq0951hu2ie1v85sbdpfsjvam@4ax.com> <13kru8ae0i51o59@corp.supernews.com> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <78254d1e-eccf-4954-929b-dbb123c4cddb@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <uoA4j.30124$Pv2.8309@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <edc2bf5a-c795-4202-92d0-2a4f46705c58@w40g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <L4_5j.5690$C24.2518@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <UGL6j.950$m6.866@newsfe18.lga> <26T6j.34409$K27.29384@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <8678e98e-8dc2-46ac-bea2-c80c8ea71d56@r60g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <WMfaj.63$R55.20@newsfe13.lga>`

```


"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote in message 
news:WMfaj.63$R55.20@newsfe13.lga...
>
> Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> withdrawing!

I admire your patience, Peter. :-)

Because there is a natural cycle of glacier advance and retreat, and solid 
data from the past is hard to come by, it is pretty hard to show that what 
is happening currently is anything out of the ordinary. Nevertheless, 
proponents of the position that GW is not serious, or that we don't know it 
is, SHOULD be looking for evidence to support that position and your very 
reasonable request should have been answered ere now.

Certainly some reference to such phenomena could sway this particular 
argument for those of us still undecided. Given that it hasn't been 
forthcoming, I thought I'd have a look myself (having used the internet 
since it had 14,000,000 people on it, I can manage search engines fairly 
well)... While it is almost impossible to come up with a list of glaciers 
that are advancing currently (and even if you did, it may have no 
significance), it is certainly possible to see some of the scientific 
thought that is going into the question.

Here's what I came up with:

http://www.ingentaconnect.com/content/igsoc/jog/2004/00000050/00000171/art00009

http://www.21stcenturysciencetech.com/2006_articles/IceCoreSprg97.pdf
I liked this one very much... to whet the appetite of forum readers, here is 
a short quote from the preface:

"When climate science was not driven by ideology, it was
generally assumed that long-term astronomical cycles-those
measured in tens or hundreds of thousands of years-were the
way in which climate had to be situated. The long 100,000-
year cycles of Ice Ages are determined by the periodicities in
the eccentricity, tilt, and precession of the Earth's orbit; in be -
tween Ice Ages, there are roughly 10,000-year periods known
as interglacials, when relatively milder climates prevail. Right
now, the Earth is at the tail end of an interglacial and probably
already entering a period of glacial advance.
The Ice Ages of the past and the coming Ice Age have a
timetable of their own, quite independent of man's industrial
output of carbon dioxide. No scientist who knew these astro -
nomical cycles could possibly be trapped into worrying about
the ups and downs of local or global temperatures in time
spans of years or even decades, or seriously be concerned
with short-term computer modelling and associated scare sto -
ries about global warming.
The times have changed, and so has environmental ideol -
ogy-but the long-range climate cycles have not changed.
This means, that based on the last several million years of his -
tory, the world is inexorably moving into another Ice Age, no
matter how much propaganda is generated about global
warming. The global warming hypothesis and the many re -
search artifacts it has generated can be dissected and dis -
proved one by one; but the fact remains that the overall
question of climate must be situated in a long view of history,
not the short term."

http://www.springerlink.com/content/a416240577228g67/
This one costs to read...

http://climatesci.colorado.edu/2006/06/18/248/

http://scienceandpublicpolicy.org/sppi_originals/current_issues_in_climate_science_focus_on_the_poles/page-9.html

Search terms for GOOGLE: glacier advance ice increase

I read several dozen of these articles and the only conclusion I can reach 
is that the case is not proven either way.

It remains a highly controversial topic...

Pete.
```

- **Date:** 2007-12-19T20:56:48-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kgljm394leaod12il4u0ajc4uqa9aj9akt@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <uoA4j.30124$Pv2.8309@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <edc2bf5a-c795-4202-92d0-2a4f46705c58@w40g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <L4_5j.5690$C24.2518@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <UGL6j.950$m6.866@newsfe18.lga> <26T6j.34409$K27.29384@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <8678e98e-8dc2-46ac-bea2-c80c8ea71d56@r60g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <WMfaj.63$R55.20@newsfe13.lga>`

```
On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 15:10:10 -0600, "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:

>
>Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>withdrawing!

The press is telling only one side of the story. We hear about decreased ice in the north
but nothing about increased ice in the south.

The entire Antarctic ice pack is increasing at about the same rate Arctic ice is
decreasing. Sea levels are not affected when floating ice in the Arctic melts. They are
LOWERED when water moves from the ocean to land in the Antarctic. 

"In their SciencExpress article, Curt Davis (University of Missouri-Columbia) and his
collaborators used satellite radar altimetry measurements from 1992 to 2003 to determine
that, on average, the elevation of about 8.5 million square kilometers of the Antarctic
interior has been increasing (Figure 1). The increasing elevation was then linked to
increases in snowfall, which was translated into a mass gain of 45 ï¿½ 7 billion tons per
year, tying up enough moisture to lower sea level by 0.12 ï¿½ 0.02 millimeters per year.

This 0.12 millimeters is a very fortuitous number. In 2000, NASA iceman William Krabill
grabbed global headlines by claiming that melting in the worldï¿½s other big
iceboxï¿½Greenlandï¿½was raising sea level by 0.13 millimeters annually. "
http://www.worldclimatereport.com/index.php/2005/05/27/antarctic-ice-a-global-warming-snow-job/
```

- **References:** `<5qnnnvF10lia4U1@mid.individual.net> <d5ork3l3ndq0951hu2ie1v85sbdpfsjvam@4ax.com> <13kru8ae0i51o59@corp.supernews.com> <846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <78254d1e-eccf-4954-929b-dbb123c4cddb@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <uoA4j.30124$Pv2.8309@newssvr23.news.prodigy.net> <edc2bf5a-c795-4202-92d0-2a4f46705c58@w40g2000hsb.googlegroups.com> <L4_5j.5690$C24.2518@newssvr17.news.prodigy.net> <UGL6j.950$m6.866@newsfe18.lga> <26T6j.34409$K27.29384@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <8678e98e-8dc2-46ac-bea2-c80c8ea71d56@r60g2000hsc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Alistair wrote:
>
> Judson, I realise that my belief in GW is as strong as your scepticism
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> sea level).

You're railing against the tide. You can't get the world to cooperate, so 
you might as well accept the inevitable (whatever it is).

As for global warming, there are no observable, unequivocal, facts. There 
are, however, unequivocal computer models (which don't really agree with 
each other). Here are some points you may have overlooked:

* The 2007 hurricane season was the third lightest in 40 years.
* The 2006 hurricane season had not one make U.S. landfall.
* Buenos Aires had its first snowfall since 1918
* The Peruvian government declared a state of emergency over cold-related 
crop failures.
* Johannesburg, South Africa, had the first significant snowfall in 26 
years.
* Australia experienced the coldest June ever.
* In New Zealand, cold weather endangered vinyards.
* In January, a five-day freeze in California wiped out $1.42 billion in 
agricultural produce. A disaster declaration was made by the president.
* 95% of Georgia's peach crop was destroyed by cold, 90% of N.Carolina's 
apples.
* In June, Denver recorded the lowest temperature ever, going back to 1872.
* December 4th in Seoul was the lowest on record (-5C)
* The Canadian government decreed this winter to be the coldest ever.

So, we have measurable facts observable to the naked eye or computer models. 
Take your pick.
```

```


"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message 
news:8678e98e-8dc2-46ac-bea2-c80c8ea71d56@r60g2000hsc.googlegroups.com...
> On 9 Dec, 14:38, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "tlmfru" <la...@mts.net> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> sea level).

I believe that to be extremely unlikely. (Even if you last until 2050, you 
will be so busy having your poultice changed, you won't notice where the sea 
level has risen to...:-))

Enlisting the support of the entire world for something that is still very 
arguable (witness the argument that has been raging here...) is probably 
going to be "difficult", so why not try for something smaller and more 
practical...?

Maybe you could start by persuading your "friend" not to chiuck his garbage 
over the fence...?

It's the little things that eventually make a real difference...

>
> As for name calling? Calling you a worshipper of a minor Midianite
> storm god is an accurate observation. Look up where Yahweh came from
> on Wikipedia for yourself and you will see what I am getting at.

Is substituting Wikipedia as your "Argument from Authority" any better than 
Judson using the Holy Bible as his?

How is Judson's (or your own) views on Religion pertinent to an argument 
about GW?

Fact is, you HAVE been using ad hominem, old fruit, and it doesn't help your 
case. Trying to push someone's buttons MIGHT be considered legitimate in 
rhetoric, but not in reasoned argument (if you are serious about your 
position) and it can only work as long as doing so does not expose buttons 
of your own... The more you beat this particular drum (and Judson fails to 
respond in kind) the worse your argument looks. It becomes apparent that the 
person REALLY obsessed about this Religion thing is not him, but you...

My advice: let it go and focus on your real arguments for GW. (If you really 
are serious and passionate about it; if you're not, and this is just a ploy 
to fill time in a boring life, then fine, blaze away... :-))

>By
> the way, It was a novel by James Branch Cabell that appraised me of
> that one.

It is wise not to base your beliefs on fiction, even though fiction can 
often stimulate serious thought... :-)

Pete.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tx_4j.10464$vt2.6921@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <78254d1e-eccf-4954-929b-dbb123c4cddb@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tsc8l355fbhugl44155asqlvni1d33a6ft@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
> So if someone tries to persuade me to believe that Brahma is the
> creator of the universe and someone else tries to persuade me that it
> is Allah - what criteria do I use to determine which way to go?

That's a good question. The Bible says that God's "invisible attributes
are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made" (Romans
1:20). I would look for a God for Whom that statement is true. A God
Who's attributes are consistent with the creation of the universe we see.
It is logical to assume that if God created the universe, that it would
be consistent with His nature. If somebody is advertising a god that is
not consistent with that, then it isn't the real deal.
```

Howard Brazee wrote:
>
> Ahh, then if the universe is all powerful and perfect, I should expect
> its maker to be all powerful and perfect.    If the universe is a
> flawed work in progress, then I should expect its maker to be a flawed
> work in progress.

You can't build a house without creating sawdust. Most religious traditions 
hold that it is man's job to improve and perfect this world, the implication 
is that the world is not as good as it can get.

This can be easily proved: If someone is hungry, I can provide a sandwich. 
If someone is displaced, I have a couch. If someone needs a job, I can make 
some calls. In other words, I can do something today to make this a better 
world.

For example, to conserve our planet, I'm promoting whale oil as a renewable 
resource.

"It's a shame to not use a flame" is our group's motto.

Ambergris is a bonus.
```

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> available WITHOUT REFERENCE TO CHRISTIAN DOGMA?

Once again, you leap to put words that I didn't say into my mouth. :-)
My reference to the Bible was simply a starting point for the thought,
not a proof text for it. I'm not so abysmally stupid as you suppose,
and I realize that a person who does not accept the Bible as God's
Word is not going to believe something based on what the Bible says.

My point, which is an easy one to come to without reference to any
specific religion, is that, if there is a God who created the universe,
then it is reasonable to assume that the nature of the universe would
be consistent with the nature of its Creator. We can see the universe
and know many things about it. And if someone advertises a god
that is not consistent with the creation of the universe you see, then it
is only reasonable to assume that the god in question is not the real
Creator. See? No reference to any specific religion. I tried to point that
out above ("It is logical to assume..."). If you hadn't been frothing at
the mouth (e.g. all caps 'shouting') trying to take issue with Christianity,
you would have seen it clearly. Chill out, dude, I'm not trying to force
Christianity on you, no reason to be bellicose. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Nature of God and Logic WAS: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-10T15:59:26+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5s3oceF179rikU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <78254d1e-eccf-4954-929b-dbb123c4cddb@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tsc8l355fbhugl44155asqlvni1d33a6ft@4ax.com> <Tx_4j.10464$vt2.6921@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <99270335-63e5-4ab3-baab-7798184f1663@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <ax_5j.9915$k27.4535@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <UsC6j.53539$_H4.32833@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```


"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message 
news:UsC6j.53539$_H4.32833@fe08.news.easynews.com...
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
> news:i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> need/want an ansewr, but I had to ask <G>)

Well, I've been too busy recently to follow this enormous thread, however, 
there are posters in CLC whose stuff I always try to read (unless a thread 
has wandered to the point where it is simply boring and has been done to 
death) and Bill is one of them. Despite not requiring an answer, Bill, it is 
a rainy afternoon here and I have some time available so... :-)

Before going into logical refutations for the existence of God, I'll try to 
address your question simply and directly.

Early Humans realised there was much in the world that was beyond their 
control. They ascribed these effects to a Supreme Cause. Thus was born the 
oldest Religion on the Planet.  Fatalism. The "Will of God".

Logically, Fatalism has a single "truth value": Always "TRUE".  Therefore it 
is impossible to argue against it logically, because it can never be 
"wrong"... For example, if I believe in Free Will and cite examples of 
choices I made which affected my Life, a Fatalist will simply say that 
whatever I "chose" was pre-determined by the Will of God and that is ALL 
there is.

As time passed, some thinkers (in particular, Aristotle) realised that any 
particular attribute of anything represents a dichotomy (hot/cold, 
light/dark, good/evil, and these dichotomies can be represented by a two 
valued system: TRUE and FALSE. (Aristotle didn't see it in quite those terms 
and I have taken certain liberties with what he DID see; nevertheless he 
certainly realised the nub of what I'm saying...)

That gave two values for everything and permitted the development of formal 
Logic. But where did this leave God? By now, Religions with multiple Deities 
had sprung up in response to the stresses of "modern" (or, perhaps more 
accurately: "communal") life, with different "departments" of Life being 
allocated to different Gods. (The urge for a Civil Service is very ancient 
in Humans; we need someone to blame when things go wrong...:-))

Throughout our recorded history, thinkers challenged the Religion as much as 
they dared and many died for it. Galileo famously recanted his "heresy" when 
hot irons and thumbscrews were hinted at (a very pragmatic and wise 
decision), but maintained his belief once outside the Star Chamber. (It 
ended up with him only being saved by influential friends and spending most 
of his life under house arrest, with his research and thoughts being 
secretly smuggled to printers outside Italy, and much not being available 
until after his death. Emmanuel Kant used Logic (which by now was a 
recognised Science, being taught in Universities) to pose his famous 
syllogism:

1. There is Evil in the World.
2. If there is Evil in the World, it is because God either can't do anything 
about it, or doesn't care about it.
3. Therefore God is either not omnipotent, or He is not a merciful caring 
God.

(You'd think this would be fairly easy to refute (for example, an 
omnipotent, caring God, might simply be choosing to allow a degree of chaos 
and sadness, as part of a greater plan that Man is not privy to... and so 
on...), but it took the Church several hundred years, and Kant came within a 
whisker of being barbequed for it. Ironic, given that he was actually quite 
a devout man...).

By the 19th century, a  (kind of...) new science of Engineering had emerged, 
and it was not interested in black and white two-valued logic. Engineers 
recognised that to build structures and engines things had to be within 
tolerences and ranges. A three-valued system? (YES, NO, MAYBE?). Well, not 
quite, but definitely more than two simple values. Applying this new logic 
system to Religion and Morality (which was the primary practical use for 
Religion), we see that there are shades of grey. The absolutes of 
Aristotelian logic become more blurred and things are relative to each 
other. (For example, killing someone is worse than stealing from them, which 
is worse than swearing at them, and God must recognise this... The Religions 
reflected "degrees" of sin...)

(In the East, where they had never heard of Aristotle, they had had a 
multi-valued Logic system based on dichotomies, with "relative" degrees of 
"right" and "wrong" (TRUE/FALSE) for millennia.)

In the 21st century, using the primitive evolving tools for thinking 
outlined above, Man finally came to adolescence in terms of thinking. The 
rate of growth of knowledge and thought sprang forward exponentially. 
Completely new sciences emerged which addressed questions which had 
previously been the sole realm of Religion. 20th century sciences (like 
Psychology and the understanding of Human behaviour) took unprecedented 
leaps and bounds forward.

More people than ever before questioned the grounds for Religion. 
Agnosticism (whether acknowledged or not) started to win the zero-sum game 
for the hearts and minds of men, and Church attendences began declining as a 
consequence.

No longer happy with Mystery and Dogma, equipped to draw their own 
conclusions from analysis of Religious books, more and more people ended 
their Agnosticism in favour of Atheism. Education, better tools for 
thinking, better understanding of Logic and thought processes, including why 
we had to invent Religion, all wrote the death of Religion in large letters 
on the wall.

But, Education is still a privelege for most of the inhabitants of the 
planet. For every literate Human Being on Earth, there are still around 3 
people who are not (Unesco figures extrapolated from a non-Global sample). 
Religion will not be going away any time soon. (Sadly, millions of people, 
as yet unborn, will die in the name of it, just as millions have done in the 
past...)

The arguments presented by Atheists like Professor Richard Dawkins are 
pretty hard to refute. (I would recommend "The God Delusion" to any Agnostic 
or uncommited religious person; it is a powerful case against Religions in 
general and doesn't just pick on Judaeo/Christian belief.) It is arguably 
possible to disprove the existence of God on logical grounds alone, but 
there are still many reasons why people cling to their imaginary friend.

I am personally satisfied that Logic is not one of them.

Pete.
```

opened INPUT and attempting to read a
record that is locked by another run-unit?

MF allows this (against the standard).
Others?

Roger

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: READ on a locked record

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-10T17:59:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<V7f7j.58923$KC6.5299@fe07.news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<846sk3lb90lqamjaijfh71c60g9hn214eg@4ax.com> <5r7o4uF13dhd6U1@mid.individual.net> <ZMA3j.19766$K27.16492@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <5r9a0vF13gqj9U1@mid.individual.net> <tCT3j.12379$rc2.1247@bignews1.bellsouth.net> <vQY3j.673$hO7.667@newsfe13.lga> <V4_3j.5902$N67.4014@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <78254d1e-eccf-4954-929b-dbb123c4cddb@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com> <ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tsc8l355fbhugl44155asqlvni1d33a6ft@4ax.com> <Tx_4j.10464$vt2.6921@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <99270335-63e5-4ab3-baab-7798184f1663@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <ax_5j.9915$k27.4535@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <UsC6j.53539$_H4.32833@fe08.news.easynews.com> <5s3oceF179rikU1@mid.individual.net> <fjjm20$k17$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Roger,
   Be careful when you say "the Standard".  In this case, I believe you are 
referring to the ISO 2002 Standard. (and the '85 Standard, which is the only 
ANSI/ISO Standard that has any "fully conforming implementations" didn't have 
file-sharing/record-locking at all.)

 However, consider the fact that the X/Open CAE specifically says (and this is 
NOT in the '02 ISO Standard),

Page 137
    "1. For files opened for input, READ or READ WITH LOCK statements will not 
acquire a record lock."

Page 138
    "Application Usage
Currently a READ statement without implicit or explicit lock, and this includes 
all READ statements for a file opened for input, may not detect a record lock 
(see Appendix A, Future Directions)."

Page 173
    "When a (sequential) READ, without explicit or implicit lock, encounters a 
locked record, it is currently implementation-dependent whether the statement is 
successful. X/Open hopes to resolve this issue in the future by defining either 
unsuccessful or successful completion of the statement."

   * * *

As the X/Open specification FAR pre-dates the ISO 2002 Standard and most of 
those implementations that currently have file-sharing/record-locking actually 
did so in conformance to (and sometimes based on) the X/Open specification, this 
explains differences between the '02 Standard and current implementations - as 
well as differences among current implementations.
```

- **References:** `<ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tsc8l355fbhugl44155asqlvni1d33a6ft@4ax.com> <Tx_4j.10464$vt2.6921@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <99270335-63e5-4ab3-baab-7798184f1663@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <ax_5j.9915$k27.4535@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> (usually they see the world as being closer to their views of Satan).

Howard, if you read my original comments, what I actually said was:

    "It is logical to assume that if God created the universe, that it
    would be consistent with His nature."

That *is not* the same as saying God has "the same" characteristics as
this universe, which you attributed to me above. Humans don't have
"the same" characteristics as a hand tool, but hand tools are designed
with human attributes in mind. The fact that screwdrivers exist implies
that their creators (humans) wanted them. For example, why would
we see such consistency in physical laws, if their creator was not also
consistent? Or, why would God create love or beauty, if He did not
posess and appreciate them? It is illogical to assume otherwise. We may
not be able to prove that such is the case with God, but it is logical
to assume so, which is all that I suggested. If the Bible is correct,
then the effects of sin and the curse in Genesis 3 are quite sufficient
to explain evil and other negative attributes.

<aside>
In formal proof, you often test a hypothesis by making it a postulate,
then following the logic to see if there are any contradictions within
the framework of the hypothesis. If one is to test the logical validity
of the Bible in the same way, you cannot use arguments that violate the
Bible as a postulate. Any contradictions must be found within the Bible
framework, just as would be the case for any other hypothesis. And when
any logically valid explaination is found within that framework for any
apparent contradiction, that is sufficient. Almost any hypothesis, even
many that have been formally proven true, can be "invalidated" by using
arguments that violate the postulates. This is only valid if the argument
is proven true and applies to the postulates. Since there is no (and can
be no) proof that God does not exist (can't prove a negative), this does
can not apply here.
</aside>
```

consistent with nature, I agree.   But it isn't consistent with the
way most Christian churches describe Him.   (The Old Testament doesn't
really mean it when it calls Him "Terrible").

Still, which characteristics of the world indicate that Jehovah is a
more likely choice than Brahma?

On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 10:35:53 -0800 (PST), Alistair
<alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>As for the Bible, the four gospels contradict each other. So which one

>more evidence do you need to realise the fallacy in your holy
>manuscript and to suspect that the other texts within the Biblke may

http://timesofindia.indiatimes.com/World/Vatican_changes_nativity_scene/articleshow/2626604.cms

 LONDON: The traditional nativity scene built each Christmas in front
of St Peterï¿½s Basilica has shown Jesus being born in a stable in
Bethlehem for 25 years. But, this year, the Vatican will do away with
the manger.

The Vatican has decided to abandon the traditional stable and the
straw-ladden setting, shifting it to Nazareth, and placing Jesus in
his father Josephï¿½s carpentry shop in a bid to reflect the more
straightforward scenario as described by St Matthew.

"Itï¿½s time for a change and a return to St Matthewï¿½s gospel," Daily
Telegraph quoted a spokesman of the State Department of the Vatican,
which organises and builds the giant presepe, or the nativity scene,
as saying.

"In fact, in place of the sheep and hay, there will be a model of
three rooms. Jesus will lie in Josephï¿½s shop, complete with the
typical work tools of a carpenter...On one side, the shop will be
flanked with a covered patio, on the other thereï¿½ll be the inside of a
pub, with its hearth." The new setting was inspired by two verses in
St Matthewï¿½s gospel, Chapter 1:24 and 1:25, the Vatican said, which
state: "When Joseph woke up, he did as the Angel of God ordered and
took Mary into his house. Without them knowing each other, a child was
born and he called his name Jesus."

But, a decision has been made to place the nativity scene in Nazareth
regardless, the spokesperson said. The traditional depiction of Jesus
in a manger comes from St Lukeï¿½s gospel, which said there was no room
at the innï¿½. But it is Matthewï¿½s gospel which forms the basis for the
Angelus prayer, and the view of Jesus in a carpenterï¿½s workshop
matches the Franciscan tradition. The nativity scene at St Peterï¿½s for
Christmas was started by Pope John Paul II in 1982.

carpenter.  The Greek word used to describe Joseph in the Bible,
takov, means "works with hands".  Forensic biblical scholars believe
Joseph more likely did odd jobs and was a stone mason and not a
carpenter.

Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com...
> On 10 Dec, 16:45, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Godel proved that in any system of logic you can make statements that can't
be proved or disproved using only the postulates in that system.  As
Alistair points out below, the Bible isn't self-consistent so more than just
the one postulate is necessary if you're going to claim the logical validity
of it.  And you will very soon come up with contradictions.  I have often
challenged people of your belief to come up with their complete set of
postulates vis a vis the Bible: I have never had a reply.


> As for the Bible, the four gospels contradict each other. So which one
> is right and which three are fabrications/tissues of lies? How much
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > arguments that violate the postulates. This is only valid if the
argument
> > is proven true and applies to the postulates. Since there is no (and can
> > be no) proof that God does not exist (can't prove a negative), this does
> > can not apply here.
> > </aside>
>

If a person claims to be a logical and honest thinker, the statement that
you can't prove a negative is a copout and is wholly unscientific.  It is
always possible, so far as I can tell, to restate the problem as "X CANNOT
exist".  In this particular case, if you will define God in a way that we
all agree on, then we can debate whether she/he/it can or cannot exist.
(You believe in God, so you have to provide the definition).  If X can't
exist, then X does not exist.  QED.

PL

"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
> The best evidence for the non-existence of God is the lack of
> substantiatable evidence for his/her/its existence.

All I have to say is, anyone who actually thinks dirt spontaneously formed
itself into the multitude of life we see has no clue about the second law of
thermodynamics, one of the most fundamental, easily demonstrable and
intuitive laws of physics. It takes *intelligent* application of energy to
create order like DNA. There is *not one single example* where anything
remotely similar has *ever* been observed to happen by "accident". E.g.
a machine (or any self contained energy system), left to itself, will run
down and deteriorate over time, *never, ever* the reverse. Automobiles
rust and wear out, they *do not* get better on their own! Q.E.D. End of
argument. Anything more is an abysmal waste of time, trying to argue with
someone who denies the consistent experience of every single human to
ever walk the planet, and denies physics from the ground up. :-)

If we went to Mars and found a machine of an order and complexity of a
bacterial flagella motor, it would be *unquestioned* evidence that it was
created by intelligence. Yet atheists gloss over them, even human beings,
and say "they got here by accident". Right! :-)

You ask for evidence? Look in a mirror; *you* are evidence! There are
many attributes in a human that Darwin himself specifically stated would
prove his theory of evolution wrong. Sexual reproduction, the human eye,
love, music, etc. *could not* have been caused by an evolution process,
even if one existed! (Psalm 14:1)
```

- **References:** `<ZZh4j.6677$k27.3423@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <bcc8fb64-062e-47bf-81d3-af367483df82@v4g2000hsf.googlegroups.com> <tsc8l355fbhugl44155asqlvni1d33a6ft@4ax.com> <Tx_4j.10464$vt2.6921@bignews8.bellsouth.net> <99270335-63e5-4ab3-baab-7798184f1663@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <ax_5j.9915$k27.4535@bignews2.bellsouth.net> <8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> either statement more precise.

Many systems *locally appear* to reverse entropy (e.g. air conditioning),
but they *always* increase total entropy in the whole system.

Since entropy works probabilistically, yes it is *theoretically* possible you
could put a pan of cold water on a hot stove and the water get colder. But
the probability of it happening makes "astronomical" look like unity.
```

Donald

- **References:** `<8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> donald tees <donaldtees@execulink.com> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> All the way down.

Not so. We live in a causal universe. For something to happen or exist
there must be a cause. For anything to exist at all there must be a First
Cause. Without a First Cause there would be, could be, nothing.

Anyway, if you believed that, how did a "Big Bang" happen? You think
an infinitely dense, infinitely small particle that exploded to create the
universe just "came out of nowhere", and that is more logical than a
Creator? If so, you have sliced yourself to bits with Occam's Razor. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-20T12:35:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cause.

The whole point Howard, is that in a causitive universe, there must be
a First Cause *external* to that universe for it to exist. The universe
could not have created itself from nothing, because nothing does nothing.
Therefore, the First Cause cannot be part of the universe, and was not
created, could have no beginning, and must be eternal, ever existing.
I.E. God. It is a logical necessity. Postulating a Big Bang is just a
logical "slight of hand", an effort to "push back" the inevitable
paradox to the point where you don't think about it. A Creator might
have caused a Big Bang, but no Big Bang happened from nothing. This is
the same trick evolutionists use when they try to use cosmic seeding to
avoid the problem of autogenesis. For life to exist, you only have two
possibilities: Creation or autogenesis; there is no other alternative.

>>Without a First Cause there would be, could be, nothing.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> something without a cause which I will call 'The Big Bang'".

Same thing. You don't have to state that everything in the universe has a
cause, it's a fact. You can't arbitrarily exempt something just because
it might be convenient. :-)

> Does the Bible say everything must have a cause?

Science says that. Plus logic and all human experience. Without causality,
there could be nothing. For anything to exist, there must be some sort of
structure or basis for that existence. Without causality, there is none.
Physical laws imply causality; without causality, there can be no physical
laws, no energy, no space, no universe, nothing. This alone proves that
no First Cause could be physical. You cannot escape that it is a logical
absolute that if *anything* exists, there *must* be a God, a First Cause,
a Creator Who is external to the universe, and *all things* were created
by that First Cause.
```

"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:wYzaj.46462$L%6.31493@bignews3.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>
So God didn't need to be created because He exists "outside" the Universe 
and is therefore not subject to causality?

If He's outside it, how can he interact with it? If He interacts with it, 
how can he NOT be subject to it?

Whether He exists inside it or outside it, where did He come from? Did he 
spontaneously spring into existence ("I AM...) and how is that any different 
from our current understanding of a Big Bang?

If He created Earth and Man so he would have someone to play with, why 
doesn't He manifest himself more often? (Last recorded interview 3500 years 
ago with Moses...)

Why would he create such a vast universe if He is only interested in Man as 
subjects to give Him adulation? (He could have stopped at the solar system 
and got pretty much the same result...)

The "logic" you describe above is flawed because it presumes causality (we 
know there are particles that violate causality and travel through negative 
time (pi-mesons and even electrons under certain circumstances...)), and 
because you evade the question of God's causality by placing Him outside the 
framework, exactly as Howard pointed out.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 41)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <85glm3p5fbg9qin4i0hpig28l9veg722a2@4ax.com> <wYzaj.46462$L%6.31493@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0melF1b47hjU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> If He's outside it, how can he interact with it? If He interacts with it, how can he NOT be subject to it?

Come on, Pete. You're outside the program you just wrote. How can you
interact with it? Duh!

> Whether He exists inside it or outside it, where did He come from? Did he spontaneously spring into existence ("I AM...) and how 
> is that any different from our current understanding of a Big Bang?

He didn't "come from" anywhere, He always was. Things must "come from"
somewhere when they're part of a finite universe. God created the universe,
and isn't part of it. He created the universe's laws, He isn't bound by them.

> If He created Earth and Man so he would have someone to play with, why doesn't He manifest himself more often? (Last recorded 
> interview 3500 years ago with Moses...)

He does manifest Himself, all the time, to those who know Him.

> Why would he create such a vast universe if He is only interested in Man as subjects to give Him adulation? (He could have stopped 
> at the solar system and got pretty much the same result...)

Who says God didn't create any other intelligent life in the universe? The Bible
doesn't address that issue at all. God did create vast numbers of spirit beings,
like angels.

> The "logic" you describe above is flawed because it presumes causality (we know there are particles that violate causality and 
> travel through negative time (pi-mesons and even electrons under certain circumstances...)), and because you evade the question of 
> God's causality by placing Him outside the framework, exactly as Howard pointed out.

How and why those particles work is not currently understood. Saying they
violate causality at this point makes no sense. Only when you understand
something can you say what the parameters are. Anyway, it's action at a
distance and zero time transfer of information that's at issue, not causality.
```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> > "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> could not have created itself from nothing, because nothing does nothing.

So there is nothing contradictory in the idea that the universe has always
existed, utterly empty or not.  The Big Bang that (for the sake of argument)
started our universe need not have been the only one.  In fact, it makes
more sense to me that there has been and will be an infinite number of big
bangs creating an infinite number of universes - from eternity to eternity -
than that there should have been just ONE which started in a totally empty
universe which itself didn't exist until the ultimate instant before the
ylem particle manifested itself - or even at the same time, I suppose.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 39)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t6Aaj.46467$L%6.3380@bignews3.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <009lm3t47v09v8qbti0ies5he30edeen98@4ax.com> <0Cyaj.46414$L%6.11571@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <jxzaj.8162$E01.4821@newsfe22.lga>`

```
"tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> ylem particle manifested itself - or even at the same time, I suppose.

An eternal universe has long been discarded, even by science. Not even
matter or plasma is thought to exist forever. Hypothesizing a continual
series of Big Bangs is nothing more than a contrivance, an effort to explain
away a Creator, nothing more. There is not one shred of evidence to back
it up.

Nothing is not "utterly empty", it is nothing, no space, no time, no energy,
no matter (just energy in another form anyway), no dimension, no universe,
nothing. Nothing does nothing. You can assume nothing created everything
or an intelligent Creator created everything. Considering that no human has
ever observed nothing create anything, Occam's Razor says we should
assume a Creator. If you want to assume otherwise, be my guest. Just don't
tell me it's logical. :-)
```

```

Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message
news:t6Aaj.46467$L%6.3380@bignews3.bellsouth.net...
> >
> An eternal universe has long been discarded, even by science. Not even
> matter or plasma is thought to exist forever. Hypothesizing a continual
> series of Big Bangs is nothing more than a contrivance, an effort to
explain
> away a Creator, nothing more. There is not one shred of evidence to back
> it up.
>

So at some time to come, time itself will stop?  Otherwise, this universe
that we live in, which eventually may come to contain nothing in all its
vastness, will last forever.

> Nothing is not "utterly empty", it is nothing, no space, no time, no
energy,
> no matter (just energy in another form anyway), no dimension, no universe,
> nothing. Nothing does nothing. You can assume nothing created everything
> or an intelligent Creator created everything. Considering that no human
has
> ever observed nothing create anything, Occam's Razor says we should
> assume a Creator. If you want to assume otherwise, be my guest. Just don't
> tell me it's logical. :-)

It's easy to conceive of a universe that is empty of matter & energy but
nevertheless has dimension.  That's a pretty good definition of "nothing".

Occam's razor says no such thing.  An eternally-there universe is a much
simpler hypothesis than a creator.  God (I assume) is by your requirements
eternal; if so, I see no reason why he would have just created OUR universe
in all eternity.

You statement that "no human has ever observed nothing create anything" is
strictly speaking correct, but factually false, as it's now known that
virtual particle pairs zip into existence from nothing and disappear almost
simultaneously - but some get half-caught at the edges of black holes,
causing them to emit "Hawking Radiation".  Their existence has been
demonstrated in other ways, such as the perturbance of electron orbits.


PL

> --
> Judson McClendon       judmc@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:t6Aaj.46467$L%6.3380@bignews3.bellsouth.net...
> "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net> wrote:
>> Judson McClendon <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> tell me it's logical. :-)

If the 11 dimensional Universe hypothesis is true, the "nothing" of which 
you speak would have been much more complex than "absence of anything". 
Stresses created by interacting dimensions could have caused a rupture that 
resulted in a massive explosion, and created "something" (Space and Energy, 
eventually leading to Time and Matter...) out of the multidimensional 
nothing. Kaku covers this in "Hyperspace"...

If that theory is NOT true (Jury is still out...) then there are still other 
possible explanations and, as our knowledge increases, so will our 
understanding. My point is that it is NOT an unknowable mystery requiring an 
omnipotent divine being to makle it happen...

It has been calculated that the sum of all the matter and energy in the 
universe (including dark matter and anti-matter) comes to zero, suggesting 
that it did indeed come from "nothing"...

Pete.
```

On Thu, 20 Dec 2007 10:40:37 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>Not so. We live in a causal universe. For something to happen or exist
>there must be a cause. For anything to exist at all there must be a First
>Cause. Without a First Cause there would be, could be, nothing.

It's easy to assume that if we find a bad argument used to support a
conclusion that the conclusion must be bad too.   I wonder if there's
a name for that fallacy.

The toughest argument is one that people use to support my causes -
but are invalid arguments.   And every cause has invalid supporting
arguments.
```

Despite the Pope asking Stephen Hawking not to go there (it was considered 
the province of God) much investigation of the Big Bang has been carried out 
and understanding of how it came about is growing. Personally, I believe the 
solution is in multiple dimensions (See "Hyperspace" by Michio Kaku) or 
possibly M Theory. Sometime within the next 25 years we will have a pretty 
good insight into it.

William of Ockham can rest easy; Big Bang is an efficient theory that fits 
the observable facts very well.

Pete.

Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> William of Ockham can rest easy; Big Bang is an efficient theory that
> fits the observable facts very well.
>

Dunno.

Dilbert: "You see, Ratbert, originally the universe would fit in a 
thimble..."
Ratbert: "What would a thimble be doing there?"
Dilbert: "Well, there wasn't one..."
Ratbert: "It didn't take long to find the flaw in THAT theory."

Also Ockham's Razor has been found to be reversed in religious studies. 
Research has shown, for example, that when confronted with two 
transcriptions of Biblical text, the MORE COMPLICATED one is likely to be 
the correct rendition.

"HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Also Ockham's Razor has been found to be reversed in religious studies. Research has shown, for example, that when confronted with 
> two transcriptions of Biblical text, the MORE COMPLICATED one is likely to be the correct rendition.

True. The reason for that is because scribes who changed the text are
presumed to have done so to make it clearer, not more difficult.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

_(thread depth: 38)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-21T11:37:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5be73dc4-a057-4217-8efc-10ebe7337090@b40g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0lffF1bjsmvU1@mid.individual.net> <13mnpr0l5ouam7d@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Dec 22, 5:18 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> the correct rendition.

Where 'correct' means 'fits my dogma'.

'Religious Studies' are about supporting pre-conceived ideas and
particular dogmas. Each of the thousands of different cults would
choose a different rendition and insist that theirs were
'correct' (which is why they are separate cults in the first place).

Of course it is also why there are different transcriptions too. The
transcribers 'improved' it based on their own ideas, or the ones they
were indoctrinated with. Do that for a few centuries after stuff had
been handed down by word of mouth for a few more, then have groups
'selecting' and revising and you have no more than myths and legends
that may be based on stories about real people who have been deified.

To take a simple example: Nicolas, Bishop of Myra, did good deeds and
was beatified. Stories were told about him, and stuff made up. The
reindeer and sleigh were added in Victorian times, the current image
comes from a Coka Cola ad in the 1930s. Today (well maybe not so much
in the last 30 years) children argue about whether it is better to
post a letter or put it in the fire to go up the chimney.

Parents indoctrinate their children with this sort of nonsense because
it is a social control. "Be good because Santa can see you, it is all
written down in his book by the elves."

This is just a training ground for religion. Children don't appreciate
the length of 'lifetime', so there is a reward/punishment system
within the timescale they can understand, that of a year.

Jehovah was a local warlord that took in as subjects a bunch of
emigrants from Egypt (allegedly). He gave them some land as long as
they remained his subjects and supplied his army with 'offerings' of
sons as soldiers and food as taxes.

Just like the later Nicolas, he was deified and could do everything
and anything. It is easy to see how this happens by studying how the
Rastafarians deified Ras Tafari in much the same way (did I mention
that my grandfather was present a lion cape by Ras Tafari in 1922 ?).

If 20th century people can create a deity and a mythology around a man
who is demonstrably flesh and blood in just a couple of decades, then
think how how whacked out stories can get in a few centuries. (Note
the first writing of the Torah was around 800BC so it had several
hundred years of exageration and myth making. Plus stuff added in from
all the other religions around the area that had been based on their
own warlords, ancestors, tribal chiefs and mytholgised heroes.

'Religious Studies' may as well be looking for clues in 'Alice in
Wonderland'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT:Thanksgiving

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-21T14:39:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13mo93lpg6fci7f@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<8bc7a6ad-7cb1-4b9c-ada9-4a9d26d12b0a@o6g2000hsd.googlegroups.com> <i5B6j.18848$_m.9137@bignews4.bellsouth.net> <plnql3p2ooqdr8dm66cqjenrgfrkiulok7@4ax.com> <E2e7j.18531$N67.17873@bignews5.bellsouth.net> <9ed42f70-9ea4-4abf-a064-344e8d84534d@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net> <fuiaj.10937$pq.7300@newsfe24.lga> <OYtaj.46273$L%6.1468@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <OZSdnWW0CqZtFPfanZ2dnUVZ_hWdnZ2d@golden.net> <fi4lm31hp3ui83bm2kpk3j1m80i7r4b2ed@4ax.com> <7Wwaj.46370$L%6.29618@bignews3.bellsouth.net> <5t0lffF1bjsmvU1@mid.individual.net> <13mnpr0l5ouam7d@corp.supernews.com> <5be73dc4-a057-4217-8efc-10ebe7337090@b40g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> On Dec 22, 5:18 am, "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Where 'correct' means 'fits my dogma'.

No, "correct" means "identical to the original."

>
> 'Religious Studies' are about supporting pre-conceived ideas and
> particular dogmas. Each of the thousands of different cults would
> choose a different rendition and insist that theirs were
> 'correct' (which is why they are separate cults in the first place).

Again, no. Here I'm referring to studies based on the texts themselves, with 
reference to nothing external (except other texts).

>
> Of course it is also why there are different transcriptions too. The
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> that may be based on stories about real people who have been deified.

No, you have differing renditions and that is why there's an on-going quest 
for the original. The original text is purely an academic exercise inasmuch 
as what the original text said is actually irrelevant. What the author (God, 
Moses, etc.) MEANT is the single important factor. The text is just a 
crib-sheet.

> If 20th century people can create a deity and a mythology around a man
> who is demonstrably flesh and blood in just a couple of decades, then
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> own warlords, ancestors, tribal chiefs and mytholgised heroes.

Heh! There's a rabbinic argument that goes: "The Torah may not have been 
written by Moses. If not, it was written down by someone else, whose name, 
coincidentally, was 'Moses'. "

Besides, "written down" is accorded more emphasis than it might deserve. 
There are people alive who can recite the Talmud from memory (for those who 
are not familiar, the Talmud puts Encyclopedia Britannica to shame - and may 
even rival Wikipedia).
```

> > If 20th century people can create a deity and a mythology around a man

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Given that they acknowledge that it was first actually written down
around 800BC and the oldest existing fragments are from around 200BC.


> Besides, "written down" is accorded more emphasis than it might deserve.

> even rival Wikipedia).

It may even rival Wikipedia for 'accuracy' and 'dogma', yes.

I am sure that many can recite 'The Ancient Mariner' from memory (I
can barely manage A.A.Milne's 'The Old Sailor').

None of this makes them any more relevant.

```


"Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote in message 
news:kKgaj.45884$K27.35574@bignews6.bellsouth.net...
> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> intuitive laws of physics.

NOBODY thinks "dirt spontaneously formed  itself into the multitude of life 
we see".

It wasn't dirt; it was organic proteins and amino acids, and it wasn't 
spontaneous, it took millions of years.



>It takes *intelligent* application of energy to
> create order like DNA.

No, it doesn't. It is an iterative, evolving process governed by naturally 
selecting what works and rejecting what doesn't.

>There is *not one single example* where anything
> remotely similar has *ever* been observed to happen by "accident". E.g.
> a machine (or any self contained energy system), left to itself, will run
> down and deteriorate over time, *never, ever* the reverse. Automobiles
> rust and wear out, they *do not* get better on their own!

Evolution does not deny entropy; it simply uses energy to improve what 
exists and replicate it.


>Q.E.D. End of
> argument.

Nope. Your Quod Erat Demonstrandum is premature and based on false premises 
as shown above.

>Anything more is an abysmal waste of time, trying to argue with
> someone who denies the consistent experience of every single human to
> ever walk the planet, and denies physics from the ground up. :-)
>

Not at all.

> If we went to Mars and found a machine of an order and complexity of a
> bacterial flagella motor, it would be *unquestioned* evidence that it was
> created by intelligence.

Only by people who believe in "intelligent design"...


>Yet atheists gloss over them, even human beings,
> and say "they got here by accident". Right! :-)

If you call a constant iterating improvement across millions of years an 
"accident", yes, we do (although it is not a word I would use. Rather, I 
would say it was the accumulation of a very large number of  high 
probability events,  resulting in an outcome that was of very low 
probability. Is that an "accident"? If it is, it took a very long time to 
come to fruition, and, as the process is still going on, it may end up with 
something even more unlikely...)

The argument you are presenting is an old one and has been refuted many 
times and at length. By seeking to make it look like Evolution is saying 
that things just spontaneously occurred by "accident" you can make the case 
you are making. But that is NOT the case presented by Evolution. A huge 
number of tiny adjustments, across a VERY long period of time, can result in 
something far more complex and wondrous than was originally the case. (Ask 
any COBOL maintenance programmer.... :-)) The sum of all the adjustments 
(just like the sum of ANY number of probabilities) can result in something 
INCREDIBLY improbable (if it was supposed to happen spontaneously), just 
like your bacterial flagella, or the duck-billed platypus, or the migration 
of the Artic Tern, or the laughter of a child.

>
> You ask for evidence? Look in a mirror; *you* are evidence! There are
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> even if one existed! (Psalm 14:1)

Not only COULD they have been, they actually WERE caused by such a process. 
Again, you quote Darwin out of context (he certainly stated what would be 
required to prove his theory wrong, but it was none of the things you 
mention and so far, none of the things he listed have come to pass.)

While I respect your right to a personal opinion and belief, I require the 
same courtesy from you, and would appreciate you not presenting hackneyed 
and specious arguments that are demonstrably untrue, as support of your own 
agenda.

If you want to argue against Evolution here I'll gladly indulge you. If you 
want to argue for "intelligent design", please, be my guest, but recognise 
it has already been rejected by the Courts in your own country and not 
without good reason... You are, to use a cricketing phrase, "on a sticky 
wicket....".

Whether you are right or I am right, Judson, the fact remains that the 
Universe can get along very nicely without God, Religion, or even Mankind 
(which invented both God and Religion, in order to meet the needs of Man). 
The stars won't go out until the Laws of Physics require them to, those Laws 
are observable by us because if they were any different, we wouldn't be here 
to observe them, and "God" had nothing to do with it. :-)

Pete.
```

> "Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message

…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

I see evidence for "good" having a universal (among humans, and perhaps 
throughout all living things) basis. Perhaps that is god.  However, 
lowering that down to a grandfather figure that sits in the sky and 
makes judgments based on a closed and stagnant philosophy strikes me as 
rather shallow.

I also have no doubts, in my own mind, that philosophical constructs 
such as love and beauty are real, and have value, but then so does 
"X"(unless, of course, it is defined as integer).

I think the average religion as useless as a mathematics that only 
allows for constants, and refuses to acknowledge variables.

Donald

recent religion building upon Christianity and Judaism. Judaism takes
it's God from a Midianite storm god. So if you wish to follow a
Western style God then go for the Midianite one. Otherwise, go for
animism (as in the Celts) or whichever long suffering eastern God
takes your fancy.
