###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-17T17:51:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65abbf@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fk5j19$djs$1@reader1.panix.com> <f810a73e-cd25-43b8-ac63-10523d5a29b3@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Dec 18, 2:11 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <5soie6F1a5e4...@mid.individual.net>,
>
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> ... as both being similar 'first time in' methods?

Actually the 'EIBCALEN code' was an alternate. It used an ELSE and did
one thing _or_ the other.  To be similar it should be:

>> IF EIBCALEN = 0
>>    PERFORM 0000-HOUSEKEEPING  THRU 0000-HSK-EX
   END-IF
   PERFORM 5000-PROCESS-INPUT THRU 5000-PI-EX.

Of course one would also need to know that this archaic and arcane
system specific name has externally set value. There would,
presumably, be no setting of EIBCALEN to some other value within the
program, so it is not even similar in its use of 'first time switch'.

How strange that you think it 'similar' at all, let alone expect me to
see it as such through your decades of ingrained cruft.

Perhaps it hasn't occurred to you that the world isn't just a bigger
version of your cubicle.

But I wasn't discussing it because it was similar or not, I was asking
whether you thought that it was an example of your 'Good Style' or
not.


> [snip]
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> that's for another time, perhaps.

I am not sure why you think that my dislike of 88 levels should be a
burden on Peter.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<fk8euu$brg$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65abbf@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
In article <28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65abbf@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Dec 18, 2:11 pm, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
>> In article <5soie6F1a5e4...@mid.individual.net>,

>one thing _or_ the other.

That might be why, Mr Plinston, it was described as 'similar' and not 
'identical'.  The difference between the two might be discenred by those 
with access to an English dictionary or a bit of education.

>> To be similar it should be:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   PERFORM 5000-PROCESS-INPUT THRU 5000-PI-EX.

That, to my eye, would change it from similar - having the characteristic 
of a first-time-in test in common - to being identical.  What 
characteristics do you see in your change which prevent this?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-18T11:00:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c939e725-053e-4d3f-a118-39ef3621d5ea@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65abbf@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fk8euu$brg$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Dec 19, 1:37 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65a...@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> That, to my eye, would change it from similar -

"IF a END-IF b". is not 'similar' to "IF a ELSE b END-IF". They are
different structures. Or do you think that all code is 'similar'. You
may even want to claim that a triangle is 'similar' to a square.

> having the characteristic
> of a first-time-in test in common -

That is only because your eye has arcane codes burnt into it. Without
that there is nothing that identifies it as a 'first time-in test'. No
meaningful name to indicate what it is, no isolation of the first-time-
in code, no setting of the flag to prevent rerunning that code.

And more importantly it seems that in your code 'Process-Input' is
_not_ performed when 'Housekeeping' is. This may be a
'initialization' (with no input), or a 'first-time' (is Process-Input'
is performed from Housekeeping) or a 'after-end-of-input', or a
'between inputs'.

You see what you are used to and consistently fail to recognise that
others have different experience.

> to being identical.  What
> characteristics do you see in your change which prevent this?

No identification that this is a first-time-in flag.

No resetting of the flag to prevent re-executing the code.

And the code itself is stylistically different, with PERFORM THRU vs
PERFORM.

You haven't denied that you think of this as 'Good Style', yet it
obviously fails the clarity test, except of course to those who
already have been told what it does.

You obviously recognised that _my_ code was 'first-time-in' because I
had deliberately included the characteristics of this to make it
clear: a meaningful name, even to those who don't live with CICS;
explicit initial value; resetting the flag within the IF branch
(rather than externally or in the PERFORMed code); continuing after
the first-time-in with the every-time-in.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-12-18T19:50:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fk98ah$ksk$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
In article <c939e725-053e-4d3f-a118-39ef3621d5ea@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
Richard  <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>On Dec 19, 1:37 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:

[snip]

>> That, to my eye, would change it from similar -
>
>"IF a END-IF b". is not 'similar' to "IF a ELSE b END-IF".

Mr Plinston, I do not know what you intend by the 's around similar but 
when I used the word I intended it as 'having characteristics in common', 
as per http://m-w.com/dictionary/similar.

Both structures appear to be covered by 
<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/FRAMESET/IGY3LR10/CCONTENTS?DT=20020920180651> 
so it might be best left to The Reader to determine if common 
characteristics - hence similarity - exists.  You might want to run a 
compile or two and inspect the generated Assembley statements to see what 
that yields, as well.

DD
```

```
So, you do agree, by your use of 'both', that they are separate and
distinct structures.

 <http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/FRAMESET/
IGY3LR1...>
> so it might be best left to The Reader to determine if common

that, with several reasons that you haven't even attempted to refute.

I would be entirely surprised if they didn't both generate some sort
of code. That in itself is a similarity of sorts. But I guarantee that
the code generated by your source (on your system) would be completely
dissimilar to the code generated by my source (on my system).

As your source would not compile on my system (it's not CICS) then
that, too, represents a dissimilarity.
```

```
do. The fact that you suggest it may be useful says more about your

need to look at the assembly code generated for whichever machine the
program is required to run on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-18T12:22:34-08:00

- **Message-ID:** `<0fe5bd21-3a4b-4bc5-97e3-316d32e6b716@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65abbf@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fk8euu$brg$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Dec 19, 1:37 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
> In article <28fef15e-4fb6-475c-935c-1fd98b65a...@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>,
>
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…
> characteristics do you see in your change which prevent this?

I have actually looked up what EIBCALEN is:

"""Re: What does it mean when EIBCALEN is equal to zeros?
Answer
# 1 	The Program is Running first time and there is not transfer of
data from program to program."""

It is an initialization with no data. Hence the need for _dissimilar_
logic that bypasses the processing step when initialization is done.

Do you think that 'having data to process' is similar to 'not having
data to process' ?
```

```
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> but can you, as well, really say that you object to the test for EIBCALEN

<sorry for mid-sentence interruption, but the sentence does go on a bit... 
:-)>

No, Doc, I'm not objecting to the test for EIBCALEN being zero; that would 
be stupid. It HAS to be tested. What I'm objecting to (and I believe it was 
also Richard's objection...) is the WAY in which it is tested. It is not 
informative or immediately obvious what the test is for or why it is needed. 
To me, it smacks of the old "Well, we are a closed Brotherhood here and if 
you don't know CICS you shouldn't be maintaining our code" school of COBOL 
Priesthood. That defeats te purpose of COBOL and, at least in my opinion, 
demeans the language.

> (essentially, for those out there not familiar with the conventions of the
> Customer Information Command System, the Exec(ution) Interface Block
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> that's for another time, perhaps.

Yes, I know Richard doesn't like 88 levels and I understand why he doesn't, 
but I don't necessarily share his aversion... :-)

Pete.
```

```
On Dec 19, 10:30 am, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> <docdw...@panix.com> wrote in messagenews:fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com...
> > In article <5soie6F1a5e4...@mid.individual.net>,
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>    88 NEW-CONVERSATION VALUE ZERO.

Looking at your 88 level which has an alleged meaningful name of "New-
Conversation" I now find that it is even less of a 'first-time-in'.

The actual EIBCALEN, I had determined by scanning the documentation,
is the LENgth of some data. That alone just means 'there is some data'
or 'there is no data' without indicating first, last, between, or
random.

Now it would seem, from your name, that it may be zero on many
occasions, or is this routine CANCELled at the end of each
conversation ?

So, even with knowing what EIBCALEN is, and your interpretation of
what it means when zero, it is still not clear, without reading
further documentation, that it is Doc's alleged 'first-time-in' (when
it is actually a separate 'initialization' anyway).

It seems that 'Good Style' is 'whatever deliberately keeps code
obscure'.


> > An 88-level?  Mr Plinston has raised objections to those, as well... but
> > that's for another time, perhaps.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-20T13:21:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5stqrlF1a3u89U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <fk5j19$djs$1@reader1.panix.com> <f810a73e-cd25-43b8-ac63-10523d5a29b3@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net> <1962347d-ad5d-48ae-99c0-4fea8d487c92@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```


"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:1962347d-ad5d-48ae-99c0-4fea8d487c92@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com...
> On Dec 19, 10:30 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[82 more quoted lines elided]…
> Conversation" I now find that it is even less of a 'first-time-in'.

It wasn't I who said it was a "first time in" :-)

This field is maintained externally to your application program, by the CICS 
system. The zero length indicates a NEW COMM-AREA and this, in turn, 
indicates the beginning of a new conversation. It may or may not be a "first 
tme in" although, if it isn't, there is probably a logic error somewhere...

>
> The actual EIBCALEN, I had determined by scanning the documentation,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No. Not by CICS convention.

> Now it would seem, from your name, that it may be zero on many
> occasions, or is this routine CANCELled at the end of each
> conversation ?

It is maintained by CICS. There is a different meaning for the word 
"conversation" in a CICS environment than is normally understood by it. In 
IMS/DC it would be called "pseudo-conversational". The point is that the 
COMM-AREA is terminal dependent and is enqueued against the specific 
terminal when the program returns a response, and provided to the program 
when the user presses enter. (hence, if CICS provides a COMM-AREA with zero 
length, it is a NEW conversation.)

>
> So, even with knowing what EIBCALEN is, and your interpretation of
> what it means when zero, it is still not clear, without reading
> further documentation, that it is Doc's alleged 'first-time-in' (when
> it is actually a separate 'initialization' anyway).

No comment :-)
>
> It seems that 'Good Style' is 'whatever deliberately keeps code
> obscure'.
>

Some people may unintentionally do that... :-)

>
>> > An 88-level?  Mr Plinston has raised objections to those, as well... 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2007-12-18T23:57:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fk9mpo$e96$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>Priesthood.

Mr Dashwood, there's a bit of a problem here.  You object to 'if you don't 
know CICS you shouldn't be maintaining CICS code'... but the alternative 
to that is 'if you don't know CICS then you should be able to maintain 
CICS code'.

I know of no language which does not require a knowledge of that language 
in order to maintain it... what am I missing?  I do not know of *any* 
CICS training that does not contain a description of EIBCALEN and what its 
length has to do with processing... but it has been a few decades since I 
was introduced to the concept and perhaps things have changed.

>That defeats te purpose of COBOL and, at least in my opinion, 
>demeans the language.

It demeans a language to expect that a maintenance programmer knows 
something about it before attempting to maintain code?  Again, I see a bit 
of a problem; code is to be written - as the Anciente Wisdome has it - so 
that a programmer with two years' experience can maintain it, not zero 
years.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-20T13:43:51+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5sts69F1atn2hU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net> <fk9mpo$e96$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```


<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:fk9mpo$e96$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> CICS code'.

No, the fact is that CICS is a susbsystem and the program is in COBOL. 
Therefore, a COBOL programmer SHOULD have a fighting chance of being able to 
maintain it. I would contend that, even without having had training in CICS, 
a competent COBOL programmer SHOULD be able to see from various calls made 
by the program, how it interfaces with CICS.  (Especially if it is coded for 
the command level pre-processor; macro level is somewhat more esoteric and 
that may be why it has fallen into disuse...) You don't need to know every 
detail of every possible CICS call in order to MAINTAIN a CICS program 
written in COBOL; you might need to know a lot more in order to CREATE a 
CICS program. It isn't quite as black and white as you suggest.

This leads me to my point that there is no reason for CICS to be 
deliberately obtuse or arcane, when used in a COBOL environment. The 
standard "good practices" used to make COBOL programs more intelligible CAN 
be applied to CICS, but they AREN'T because "we've always done it that way".

>
> I know of no language which does not require a knowledge of that language
> in order to maintain it... what am I missing?

"A knowledge" is not the same as "fully trained".  While "fully trained" may 
indeed be better, in the real world, subject to real pressures, "a 
knowledge" very often has to suffice.  It can be done (I know, because I had 
to do it...:-)), but it is better if as much as possible is done ot make it 
meaningful and easy, just like any other COBOL program.


>I do not know of *any*
> CICS training that does not contain a description of EIBCALEN and what its
> length has to do with processing... but it has been a few decades since I
> was introduced to the concept and perhaps things have changed.

I bet you know a number of COBOL people who have been required to maintain 
CICS programs without having done a CICS course, though?
>
>>That defeats te purpose of COBOL and, at least in my opinion,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> years.

Well the "Anciente Wisdome" is a bit off the beam here... (maybe that's why 
we're having this discussion...:-))

A modern programmer will have had minimal COBOL and be required to pick it 
up, and if CICS is added to that mix, anything we can do to make life easier 
for him/her has to be a "Good Thing".

I am not arguing about what is DESIRABLE, rather about what ACTUALLY 
HAPPENS... (Don't let's get sidetracked into lack of training and so on...)

Pete.
```

```
>> know CICS you shouldn't be maintaining CICS code'... but the alternative
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

It would seem that a COBOL programmer does have a fighting chance... if 
that chance allows for maintaining only the COBOL part of the program and 
not the CICS.

[snip]

>This leads me to my point that there is no reason for CICS to be 
>deliberately obtuse or arcane, when used in a COBOL environment.

Mr Dashwood, I do not know how CICS is used in other environments; perhaps 
someone else here might say whether it is 'deliberately obtuse or arcane'.

As far as COBOL is concerned... learning COBOL is one thing, learning CICS 
is another.

>The 
>standard "good practices" used to make COBOL programs more intelligible CAN 
>be applied to CICS, but they AREN'T because "we've always done it that way".

... or 'because the change order doesn't cover it'... or 'because the 
testing budget doesn't cover it'... or any one of the usual 'things must 
stay the way they are because...' scenaria.

[snip]

>> I do not know of *any*
>> CICS training that does not contain a description of EIBCALEN and what its
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>CICS programs without having done a CICS course, though?

They either had a formal course or had 'apprenticeship time' with a 
programmer who had... but my experience, of course, is limited.

CICS is arcane enough that I've seen formally trained programmers with 
more than two years' experience make errors which brought down a region, 
costing a company time and money... assigning a 'virgin' to such a task 
does not seem to be the soundest decision one might make.

[snip]

>A modern programmer will have had minimal COBOL and be required to pick it 
>up, and if CICS is added to that mix, anything we can do to make life easier 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>HAPPENS... (Don't let's get sidetracked into lack of training and so on...)

Ummmm... that is no sidetracking, Mr Dashwood.  Any manager who expects 'a 
modern programmer' to pick up CICS without formal training or a period of 
'apprenticeship' with a trained programmer deserves the system crashes 
(and associated expenses) that follow.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2007-12-20T08:37:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MQpaj.62339$MJ6.60290@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<5sts69F1atn2hU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <5soie6F1a5e45U1@mid.individual.net> <fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com> <5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net> <fk9mpo$e96$1@reader1.panix.com> <5sts69F1atn2hU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> ((snip) 
> No, the fact is that CICS is a susbsystem and the program is in COBOL. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> CICS program. It isn't quite as black and white as you suggest.

I agree with you that a competent COBOL programmer should be able to 
maintain a CICS COBOL program without knowing CICS.  But the Command 
Level CICS is the only supported interface since CICS 3.x was released 
in the early 1990's.  The Macro-level interface is no longer 
supported.  Back in 1985 IBM was telling us that macro-level CICS 
would be dropped at some point in the future.

> 
> This leads me to my point that there is no reason for CICS to be 
> deliberately obtuse or arcane, when used in a COBOL environment. The 
> standard "good practices" used to make COBOL programs more intelligible CAN 
> be applied to CICS, but they AREN'T because "we've always done it that way".

Again, I agree with you, and I try to write CICS COBOL programs so 
they are understandable.  Not all CICS programs are 
pseudo-conversational, but I do know that upon entry to a CICS 
program, if EIBCALEN=0 there is no dialog input in DFHCOMMAREA.  It's 
not an error, it just means that the program must send the first 
dialog message before any User dialog input can be received.

> (snip)
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> for him/her has to be a "Good Thing".

I don't see how anyone can disagree with that, and I would argue that 
it's still a good thing even if the maintenance programmer is an 
expert in both COBOL and CICS.

> 
> I am not arguing about what is DESIRABLE, rather about what ACTUALLY 
> HAPPENS... (Don't let's get sidetracked into lack of training and so on...)
> 
> Pete.

With kindest regards,
```

```
news:5sqsffF1aacc8U1@mid.individual.net...
<snip>
> No, Doc, I'm not objecting to the test for EIBCALEN being zero; that would be 
> stupid. It HAS to be tested. What I'm objecting to (and I believe it was also 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As a personal opinion, I would say that if anyprogrammer does not INSTANTLY know 
what is happening in a COBOL program that has

  IF EIBCALEN = 0

then I would say that programmer should NOT be "touching" that specific program. 
CICS is "arcane" (or at least very specific in its "logic" and "methodology") 
and a programmer that doesn't recognize that specific code sequence almost 
CERTTAINLY is not one who should be modifying any other part of the particular 
program.

It is also worth noting that EIBCALEN is probably (almost certainly?) in a 
COPYBOOK that the application programmer cannot "play with" and add 88-levels 
to.
```

```
wrote:
> <docdw...@panix.com> wrote in messagenews:fk76nh$9se$1@reader1.panix.com...

…[34 more quoted lines elided]…

If one does not know and understand the ideosyncracies of CICS then it
would be best to not code in CICS. It is not an indictment of Cobol
but rather of CICS. If you do not know what you are doing it is a
simple matter to bring the CICS region down (well, it was a few years
ago. I doubt if anything has changed since).
```

```
The documention appears to imply it will be zero exactly once.

Peter seems to think it will be zero for the start of each
'conversation' (which I would take as a series of related interactions
with a single terminal).

Doc indicates it may "have a value" (where it seems zero is not a
value) on first entry.

And I was called 'slow' for not understanding this between messages
when everyone else is required to take 2 years.
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:cdbc40fd-a397-41ce-8deb-f8c54c629e83@d27g2000prf.googlegroups.com...
> On Dec 20, 8:52 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
>> On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 09:40:09 -0800 (PST), Alistair
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Fair comment.

It is obvious to me from the responses here that the "CULT of CICS" is not 
going to be moved so I see no point in wasting further time on this.

All I will say in closing is that I know of at least one site where Java 
programmers have been required to do some maintenance on CICS COBOL 
programs, and they did so without "bringing the region down" or causing 
mayhem and destruction, and without having a CICS course. (They had some 
discussions with a person who knew CICS).

The problem here is not CICS or COBOL but an entrnched mind set that cannot 
and will not be changed.

Fortunately (for the rest of us) it is in its Goetterdammerung... :-)

Pete.
```

```
It might be useful to have naming conventions for 88 level names that
are designed with the grep user in mind.

Maybe something like this:

05   SW-CURRENT-STUDENT              PIC X   VALUE 'N'.
      88   CURRENT-STUDENT-FOUND             VALUE 'Y'.
      88   CURRENT-STUDENT-NOT-FOUND      VALUE 'N'.
```

```
<Joke>

Using grep (or similar) is *SO* '60's.  All current COBOL sensitive editors have 
ways of searching for "referenced data items" that include 88-levels and other 
"implicit" modifications to COBOL data items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-14T12:38:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b2e74dd0-9bd5-42e5-8347-0564250036aa@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <966198db-204f-4634-9c2b-8d21439f6586@o42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <794c0dfd-a161-4f3a-b26f-a30aeb06bc9c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <PdA8j.157869$_H4.7635@fe08.news.easynews.com>`

```
On Dec 15, 7:48 am, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.netcom.com>
wrote:
> <Joke>
>
> Using grep (or similar) is *SO* '60's.

Well, actually it is late 70's.  grep derives from the Unix ed (and vi
derivative) editor's command g/re/p which means 'global / regular
expression / print', in other words a text editor search function able
to find variations described by the expression.

Having it additionally as a separate utility allows it to feed results
into any other utility and build scripts that save having to do things
manually by typing, and retyping, into a text editor.

> All current COBOL sensitive editors have
> ways of searching for "referenced data items" that include 88-levels and other
> "implicit" modifications to COBOL data items.

'All' ?  Didn't you recently critize someone for implying 'all' ?

Does the lack of this feature indicate that an editor is not 'current'
or not 'Cobol sensitive' ?

Anyway, that 'solution' is just a complexification of a problem that
is best solved (IMHO) by avoiding it.  For example, that solution
would most likely use proprietry editors that may not be available,
for various reasons, on a particular system.

I use an editor that I can adapt to whatever task that I am currently
engaged on, but sometimes I have to use different ones and adapt to
those. Tying code to an editor's feature set may make good business
sense to the editor's vendor, but not to me.
```

```
"Richard" <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> LX-i <lxi0...@netscape.net> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does it take regular expressions ?

No, and it doesn't even work consistently as a straight search. Many times
I have seen Explorer (WinXP) return "not found" when there was more than one
ASCII text file in the target directory containing the search text. I've quit
using Explorer's text search facility for this reason. The other features of
Explorer's search seem to work okay.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-12-21T22:18:01-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ontom312g3pm2nj7m0daouf927vjt5dcan@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <794c0dfd-a161-4f3a-b26f-a30aeb06bc9c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <0941ecbc-e6dd-425c-b224-148dc1e07671@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <e4ydnevDuoW6u_banZ2dnUVZ_gmdnZ2d@comcast.com> <e4omm31123kfrc09hfobjtg880sge4be6v@4ax.com> <98160deb-fab3-4de9-bbc6-62d9c9aed689@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <pjhnm39sr2ksrur18sfib2s71ac06jnb5u@4ax.com> <ronnm31ec7db5ltrmneisor87qkrllr0th@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 21 Dec 2007 08:45:55 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Fri, 21 Dec 2007 08:13:26 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>percentage of Mac users know it includes a BASH shell and VIM?

That's my point. The only Unixen that were moderately successful in the desktop world were
Apple's OS X, which does NOT use X11 at all, and Umbuntu, which emphasizes ease of use and
a prettied up GNOME-based GUI. 

When the user interface is a Web browser, the whole concept of operating system on the
user's machine becomes irrelevant. Users are frustrated by having to deal with OS issues
on their local machines. 'What do you mean I have to reinstall everything? All I did was
restore System32. Hey, I'm not a computer genius.' It was worse in the DOS days, when we
required users to deal with IRQ collisions. It was even worse in the '80s, when users had
to deal with hardware issues. 'Do you see a trace between A13 and B12? OK, cut that trace.
What do you mean, how?' 

Techies are terrible at understanding what consumers need. For examples, see any cell
phone, digital camera, printer,  VCR or cable box. The user interfaces on them were
designed by engineering techies, who are even worse than programmers.
```

```
I don't like KDE, which eliminates KFind. Beagle runs under .NET/Mono, which uses GNOME.
Its user interface looks pretty good. It can search inside structured documents such as
zip, Word and pdf, which is very good. Grep can't. Users expect that, don't understand why
it's difficult.

Actually, I think OS provided GUIs are passe. Web browser is the way to go. Google is on
the right track with Gears, but it now supports only JavaScript. When the browser is your
UI, you can run on ANY platform (except mainframe). You can run locally or through the
network. Users are already familiar with the UI.

>> Google doesn't take regular expressions either, except in the programming area. It offers
>> similar capability described so that non-programmers  can understand.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>appropriate.

You have got to be kidding. Programmers cannot generate 200 million queries per day. 
Google runs 450,000 servers.
```

- **References:** `<0941ecbc-e6dd-425c-b224-148dc1e07671@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <e4ydnevDuoW6u_banZ2dnUVZ_gmdnZ2d@comcast.com> <e4omm31123kfrc09hfobjtg880sge4be6v@4ax.com> <98160deb-fab3-4de9-bbc6-62d9c9aed689@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <pjhnm39sr2ksrur18sfib2s71ac06jnb5u@4ax.com> <df016a03-4b2c-4ff3-92ca-3ab04f125de8@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <l1rom3h01sa5vo344eg913648o8va2umo6@4ax.com> <13mq4m3rn5casdc@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sat, 22 Dec 2007 07:36:04 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Robert wrote:
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>They must be running Linux. 

Google wrote its own operating system, disk management system, and everything else. The
450,000 number came from a NY Times article, which estimated server growth as:
2001      8,000
2003  100,000
2005  450,000

Its estimates for Microsoft are 200,000 in 2006, 800,000 in 2011.
http://www.nytimes.com/2006/06/14/technology/14search.html?ei=5088&en=c96a72bbc5f90a47&ex=1307937600&partner=rssnyt&emc=rss&pagewanted=print

I think the basis for that number is:

According to Paul Strassmann's lecture, a standard Google Cluster contains 359 racks with
31654 machines.

He also mentions that there are ">12" [now 15] data centers around the globe. Assuming
each data center has at least one Google Cluster that means that Google is operating >
379848 individual machines.

http://www.strassmann.com/pubs/gmu/LectureV4.pdf
http://www.roughtype.com/archives/2006/03/a_question_for.php

One Google admirer wrote:

Rob Pike has gone to Google. Yes, that Rob Pike -- the OS researcher, the member of the
original Unix team from Bell Labs. This guy isn't just some labs hood ornament; he writes
code, lots of it. Big chunks of whole new operating systems like Plan 9.

Google doesn't deploy bare motherboards on exposed trays anymore; they're on at least the
fourth iteration of their cheap hardware platform. Google now has an institutional
competence building and maintaining servers that cost a lot less than the servers everyone
else is using. And they do it with fewer people.

Think of the little internal factory they must have to deploy servers, and the level of
automation needed to run that many boxes. Either network boot or a production line to
pre-install disk images. Servers that self-configure on boot to determine their network
config and load the latest rev of the software they'll be running. Normal datacenter ops
practices don't scale to what Google has. 

Google is a company that has built a single very large, custom computer. It's running
their own cluster operating system. They make their big computer even bigger and faster
each month, while lowering the cost of CPU cycles. It's looking more like a general
purpose platform than a cluster optimized for a single application.

This computer is running the world's top search engine, a social networking service, a
shopping price comparison engine, a new email service, and a local search/yellow pages
engine. What will they do next with the world's biggest computer and most advanced
operating system? 

http://blog.topix.com/archives/000016.html
```

```
On Sat, 22 Dec 2007 16:03:03 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:

>Robert wrote:
>> On Sat, 22 Dec 2007 07:36:04 -0600, "HeyBub" <heybub@gmail.com> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>Okay, 379,848 servers instead of 400,000. 

That estimate was based on 12 data centers. Google has 15, which bumps the number to
474,810.

>Still works out to being able to 
>handle one query every three minutes. Must be running a home-made operating 
>system.

Think of Google as a supercomputer that handles 2,315 complex transactions per second.

>For a few hundred bucks, they could install Win 2003 Server. But then, I 
>guess, they'd miss the thrill of fiddling with the OS. 

Google is full of people with PhDs in computer science. Their Brilliant Ideas tend to be
costly in cpu cycles. In my experience, they tend to write crap code. At least they don't
wear the business suit uniform, write well and have a sense of humor (EXCEPT about their
own aforementioned code).  Their projects are not vapor. Their efforts to sell us stuff
will be more artful than pop-ups a'la Web 1.0,

We've heard about their gourmet food. We don't hear about their reputation for low pay. 

Job ads from cultures like that read "degree in X or equivalent." They mean 'degree in X,
period.' There are no equivalents, they're just assuaging guilty consciences for being
dogmatic. In the case of Google and the like, X = computer science. In the case of
embedded, X = engineering. It is little known that on the '60s, a significant percentage
of engineers at NASA, HP, et al. were self-taught with no engineering degree. Today,
autodidactic engineers don't stand a chance of being hired. The same is starting to heppen
to programmers in ivory tower places like Google.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com>
- **Date:** 2007-12-22T09:34:23-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<W7abj.26881$vt2.17026@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`
- **References:** `<fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <794c0dfd-a161-4f3a-b26f-a30aeb06bc9c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <0941ecbc-e6dd-425c-b224-148dc1e07671@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <e4ydnevDuoW6u_banZ2dnUVZ_gmdnZ2d@comcast.com> <e4omm31123kfrc09hfobjtg880sge4be6v@4ax.com> <98160deb-fab3-4de9-bbc6-62d9c9aed689@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <pjhnm39sr2ksrur18sfib2s71ac06jnb5u@4ax.com> <df016a03-4b2c-4ff3-92ca-3ab04f125de8@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <l1rom3h01sa5vo344eg913648o8va2umo6@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
> You have got to be kidding. Programmers cannot generate 200 million queries per day.
> Google runs 450,000 servers.

That number, or more, appears to be the conventional wisdom since the 2006
Times article that used it. However, I find that number too large to be credible.
Tens of thousands, maybe. But hundreds of thousands, I doubt it.

As has been pointed out, 200 million queries per day / 450k servers = 444
queries per server per day. That would be a very low hourly figure, and
massive overkill, which is not good business. 450,000 servers could easily
handle 100 times that traffic (20 billion queries/day ~ 1.9 seconds per query
per server).

Just like military secrets, when people can't find out the details, they can
get a bit nutty about it.
```

```
On Sat, 22 Dec 2007 09:34:23 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <judmc@sunvaley0.com> wrote:

>"Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Tens of thousands, maybe. But hundreds of thousands, I doubt it.

This guy did calculations from Google's 2006 S-1 filing, came up with 30,000 to 80,000. I
think his estimates are low because they are based on industry norms. Google builds its
own machines for less.

http://www.tnl.net/blog/2004/04/30/how-many-google-machines/

>As has been pointed out, 200 million queries per day / 450k servers = 444
>queries per server per day. That would be a very low hourly figure, and
>massive overkill, which is not good business. 450,000 servers could easily
>handle 100 times that traffic (20 billion queries/day ~ 1.9 seconds per query
>per server).

Someone estimated the number of cpus answering queries at 10,000. That's 14 per minute.
Still way off, since the average query takes a few milliseconds.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** tim Josling <tejgcc_nospam@westnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2007-12-22T20:07:40
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<13mqrkc53rkjj66@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <794c0dfd-a161-4f3a-b26f-a30aeb06bc9c@e6g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <0941ecbc-e6dd-425c-b224-148dc1e07671@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <e4ydnevDuoW6u_banZ2dnUVZ_gmdnZ2d@comcast.com> <e4omm31123kfrc09hfobjtg880sge4be6v@4ax.com> <98160deb-fab3-4de9-bbc6-62d9c9aed689@t1g2000pra.googlegroups.com> <pjhnm39sr2ksrur18sfib2s71ac06jnb5u@4ax.com> <df016a03-4b2c-4ff3-92ca-3ab04f125de8@s19g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <l1rom3h01sa5vo344eg913648o8va2umo6@4ax.com> <W7abj.26881$vt2.17026@bignews8.bellsouth.net>`

```
On Sat, 22 Dec 2007 09:34:23 -0600, Judson McClendon wrote:

> "Robert" <no@e.mail> wrote:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> can get a bit nutty about it.

Everyone seems to be assuming that all that Google's servers do is answer
queries. Even in the query space, they have to refresh all the data
periodically and index it, which is resource intensive. One of the posts
also assumed that each query is processed by one server, which is unlikely
to be the case.

Google also runs a lot of other services such as email and maps. Testing
also uses a lot of servers, when you are testing for volume performance.

Google puts a lot of effort into getting the most bang for the buck. The
$250m they have spent on hardware, divided by $1,000 would equate to
hundreds of thousands of servers. Have a look at the size of the
numerous data centres they have put up around the world. 

There are a number of very interesting white papers on their web site,
about how they do things.

Tim Josling
```

```
>Everyone seems to be assuming that all that Google's servers do is answer
>queries. Even in the query space, they have to refresh all the data
>periodically and index it, which is resource intensive. One of the posts
>also assumed that each query is processed by one server, which is unlikely
>to be the case.

Strassmann says more than 1,000 servers are used to answer one query. The main activity is
searching a very large index in parallel.
```

```
On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 10:16:27 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Sat, 15 Dec 2007 16:09:30 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>scale as mainframes.

z/OS scales from big to very big. It cannot run on handhelds, and isn't run on
supercomputres. It isn't even economically practical on medium sized, which run VSE.

Security is mostly a function of administration, or lack thereof. If z/OS were deployed on
a hundred million home computers, some or most of them would be vulnerable. 

As for reliability, when is the last time you couldn't get dial tone?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2007-12-17T11:58:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9qgdm3d59p99dsr4dd03kb45sv46ljmcj1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <966198db-204f-4634-9c2b-8d21439f6586@o42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <7ibdm3lb3ju7jgk2fs5ev9nnvit44a0hc0@4ax.com> <ibfdm31d5jum83dumrt1umf66v9tahp4fb@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 12:33:48 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:

>>>Mainframe systems are hugely EXPENSIVE to the companies that run them.
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>supercomputres. It isn't even economically practical on medium sized, which run VSE.

Are you implying that tools should be optimized to fit their task?   
Or are you implying that one size should fit all?

A train engine won't run a cargo ship, nor will it run a sports car.
It is possible to use sports cars to carry large amounts cargo across
the country if the cargo can be compartmentalized sufficiently.   And
there is a business case for using sports cars for some deliveries.

But different business needs have different optimal tools.

It is easy to say that the solution that works for me is the Right
solution.    But lots of hatred, wars, and missed opportunities are
created by people who reject the notion that some other solution works
for others.

Trains, cargo ships, and big iron computers are soooo yesterday.
Anybody who uses them obviously must not have done a careful analysis
of fashions.

>Security is mostly a function of administration, or lack thereof. If z/OS were deployed on
>a hundred million home computers, some or most of them would be vulnerable. 

Therefore, Big Iron is dead.   We should replace it with a server farm
- because if Z/OS could be vulnerable on a hundred million computers,
obviously it isn't secure on a single secured spot.

>As for reliability, when is the last time you couldn't get dial tone?

I'm not getting why you are asking this, but last week.
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2007-12-17T12:35:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bf572609-706f-4bbc-a502-03c27e90914b@i12g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <966198db-204f-4634-9c2b-8d21439f6586@o42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <7ibdm3lb3ju7jgk2fs5ev9nnvit44a0hc0@4ax.com> <ibfdm31d5jum83dumrt1umf66v9tahp4fb@4ax.com> <9qgdm3d59p99dsr4dd03kb45sv46ljmcj1@4ax.com>`

```
On Dec 18, 7:58 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 12:33:48 -0600, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> >>>Mainframe systems are hugely EXPENSIVE to the companies that run them.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or are you implying that one size should fit all?

I think that it was you that claimed 'scale'. Linux, in fact, can and
does run on watches and some of the largest supercomputers. It is not
that it should, but it can.

> A train engine won't run a cargo ship,

I wonder why you would imagine this. The same engines have been used
in both trains and ships.

> nor will it run a sports car.

Interestingly, they have. The Bugatti Royale did use a train engine,
One of Bugatti's main business was engines for railcars.

> It is possible to use sports cars to carry large amounts cargo across
> the country if the cargo can be compartmentalized sufficiently.   And
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> obviously it isn't secure on a single secured spot.

I think it was you who claimed 'security' for mainframes. This
security is not inherent in the design but is the result of
administration.

> >As for reliability, when is the last time you couldn't get dial tone?
>
> I'm not getting why you are asking this, but last week.

IBM big iron mainframes certainly are reliable, but so are other
systems.
```

```
>> Therefore, Big Iron is dead.   We should replace it with a server farm
>> - because if Z/OS could be vulnerable on a hundred million computers,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>administration.

With all other things being equal, putting everything in one box makes
it much easier to defend against attackers.

On the other hand, if we lose that battle, we have lost all.

A server farm's design makes it easier to have redundancy.
A centralized mainframe's design makes it easier to have security.

The same thing happens all over.   Weapons in an arsenal are easier to
safeguard - but it's harder to round up all of the weapons in people's
houses.    This isn't because one has better administration than the
other, it is an inherent characteristic of centralized vs distributed
design.
```

```
On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 11:58:27 -0700, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 12:33:48 -0600, Robert <no@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>there is a business case for using sports cars for some deliveries.

I was answering YOUR claim that z/OS has superior scalability. Unix is much more scalable
AND runs on less expensive platforms.

Using your analogy, an internal combustion engine can propel everything from a bicycle to
a cargo ship.  Neither a nuclear power plant nor a  coal furnace can scale as well.. 

>It is easy to say that the solution that works for me is the Right
>solution.    But lots of hatred, wars, and missed opportunities are
>created by people who reject the notion that some other solution works
>for others.

Agreed. There's are lots of quasi-religious beliefs in the computer business.

>Trains, cargo ships, and big iron computers are soooo yesterday.
>Anybody who uses them obviously must not have done a careful analysis
>of fashions.

The topic is cost. Many people who HAVE done a careful analysis of cost dumped mainframes
in favor of Big Unix. 

>>Security is mostly a function of administration, or lack thereof. If z/OS were deployed on
>>a hundred million home computers, some or most of them would be vulnerable. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>obviously it isn't secure on a single secured spot.

I was answering YOUR claim that z/OS has superior security. 

>>As for reliability, when is the last time you couldn't get dial tone?
>
>I'm not getting why you are asking this, but last week.

Dial tone was sent by a Unix machine, which used to be in the central office. Now it's
likely to be on a telephone pole (as DLC/DSLAM) or in a closet (as PBX).  Try putting a
'scalable' mainframe on a telephone pole.
```

```
On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 14:28:07 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 15 Dec, 22:09, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Fri, 14 Dec 2007 00:11:28 +0000 (UTC), docdw...@panix.com () wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>OK Robert: Microsoft or IBM? Whom would you rather get into bed with?

If I HAD to pick one, it would be Microsoft. At least half the things they do are either
good or well intentioned. They had the courage and size to move the industry from text to
GUI screens in 1995. If it were up to IBM, we'd still be using green screens. Their OLE
and later .NET were major improvements in interface standardization, done in a
non-proprietary way. IBM would have made them proprietary.

What has IBM accomplished in the last 25 years? Tivoli and WebSphere are the only ones I
can think of (EJB was IBM's idea, but was developed by Sun). Mostly they played Me Too
with Linux so they could milk the mainframe cash cow longer.

Actually, I WAS in bed with IBM earlier this year, on an eight month project that ended in
two months. A hundred contractors were dismissed without notice. In other shops, I noticed
IBM contractors were always the first to go, because they were the most expensive. Their
bill rate is $200, of which the worker gets maybe $40. They are NOT the best; if anything,
they're below average. Often, the IBM manager doesn't even interview them. Now, IBM is
replacing 20,000 (out of 40,000) Unix machines in their Service Centers with mainframes
running Linux. How much displacement do you think that will cause? 

I'm not a Microsoft fan, but I'm not a Microsoft hater either.
```

```
On Dec 18, 6:45 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 14:28:07 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

> good or well intentioned.

Half the things they do are bought in products. They bought MSSQL from
Sybase, FrontPage from FrontPage, IE from Spyglass (except they never
paid for it).

It is only '_well_ intentioned' if the intent is to 'make more
billions' and/or drive any competition out of business [ducks chair
thrown across room].

> They had the courage and size to move the industry from text to
> GUI screens in 1995.

Microsoft has never been a leader in anything they did. Before Windows
was released the industry was already moving to GUI with Atari, GEM,
Lisa, Macintosh, PERQ, and many others.

One point to note is that in the first edition of 'The Way Ahead'
there is no mention at all of the internet. 2nd ed. corrected that
while MS tried to build an alternate to the internet, the first MSN.
When everyone ignored that and used Netscape or IBM connect on their
Windows boxes MS had to give in and follow once again.  Of course
their PR is such that many think Billy boy invented the internet.

> If it were up to IBM, we'd still be using green screens.

Excuse me, but it was IBM that built the PC. Granted it was to compete
with the Apple ][. It was IBM that brought out multi-tasking OS/2 with
Display Manager that MS stole code from to make Windows/386 work.

> Their OLE
> and later .NET were major improvements in interface standardization, done in a
> non-proprietary way. IBM would have made them proprietary.

In what way is _anything_ that MS does 'non-proprietary' ?  MS take
standards and then build in proprietary extensions to lock out
competition. eg the HTML is generated by their products in a non-
standard way so that only IE can display it correctly.

>
> What has IBM accomplished in the last 25 years? Tivoli and WebSphere are the only ones I
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm not a Microsoft fan, but I'm not a Microsoft hater either.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<5745887d-8142-4b88-a02e-0d19f5800206@e10g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<18bff75d-832d-4089-a301-1b5162d5eed4@a35g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <966198db-204f-4634-9c2b-8d21439f6586@o42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <186288e6-37e2-4512-9b7c-73b72b2d334a@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Dec 20, 6:32 am, Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> On 18 Dec, 05:45, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably still be using green screens too.

No. That is not correct.

It was Apple that went to Xerox, and paid then for the visit. They
then created Lisa. MS had previously implemented their Basic on Apple ]
[ and was asked to work on various projects on Lisa and Macintosh.

DRI meanwhile developed GEM and demonstrated a prototype running on MS-
DOS at Comdec. This pushed MS into _announcing_ Windows later at the
same Comdec, which they then started to write. Apple was a customer,
DRI/GEM was a competitor and a threat. Windows 1 was approximately on
a par with GEM prototype but was 20 months later. Windows 2 was only
slightly worse than GEM 1.

Apple sued DRI and this basically stopped GEM development. Prototypes
of GEM-X on FlexOS (DRIs realtime system) were demonstrated but
withdrawn. A million copies of GEM on MS-DOS had sold before Windows 1
was released. GEM also was the GUI of Atari 512.

There were several other GUI systems around and MS was, as usual,
following a trend and announcing vapourware to stop the market while
it bought in or wrote something.

History gets rewritten to make Bill the inventor of the transistor,
the internet and the GUI.

The winner write the history books, the losers write the songs.
```

```
On Dec 20, 11:25 am, "Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
> "Alistair" <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> MS saw Apple's stolen Xerox ideas, and copied them. :-)

Apple didn't 'steal' anything, it _PAID_ Xerox to use those ideas,
something which Microsoft omitted to do.


> Let's have a cheer for the boneheaded managers at Xerox who were so
> obtuse as to throw away GUI, windows, and the computer mouse, all
> ideas from Xerox PARC! Xerox invented them, and could have owned
> it all today.
> --

The Mouse was invented at Stamford Research Institute in 1963. Even
then it was really an inverted trackball which had been around in, eg,
radar systems for quite a while.

windows was also a result of Stamford's, though theirs were tiled (as
the much later MS Windows 1's were). Xerox made overlapping windows
for the Alto but went back to tiled for the Star.

Also the Three Rivers PERQ which was manufactured by ICL was developed
in 1979. It had a GUI similar but faster than the Alto. I was able to
play with one of these when I went to Bracknell in 1981. I still have
a set of manuals for this machine.



> Judson McClendon       ju...@sunvaley0.com (remove zero)
> Sun Valley Systems    http://sunvaley.com
> "For God so loved the world that He gave His only begotten Son, that
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life.
```

```
article he wrote on their visit to PARC. Jobs says they simply called
up the place and were invited over. No money, no contract, nothing.
The Xerox management was so clueless they thought what PARC was doing

for a change. Maybe Jobs lied, but he is one of the few people who
unquestionably knows what happened. :-)
```

```
"""Xerox was given Apple stock in exchange for engineer visits and an
understanding that Apple would create a GUI product."""

"""The delicious highlight of ''Dealers of Lightning'' recounts Jobs's
famous tour. Contrary to legend, the members of the PARC team were
well aware of the stakes. In fact, they made the Apple team wait for
three hours as, tearful and angry, they debated showing their secret
technologies. But having been promised shares in Apple's hotly
anticipated stock offering, a Xerox executive forced the issue by
phone. Jobs got his demo, folded what he'd seen into new Apple
computers and set off an exodus of PARC employees that marked the end
of the research center's heyday."""

ideas, he didn't steal them. And certainly it  wasn't Microsoft that
```

- **Date:** 2007-12-19T19:34:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vpejm35tpje1m4ivrg8m13i1jr4r14ivlu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<966198db-204f-4634-9c2b-8d21439f6586@o42g2000hsc.googlegroups.com> <fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <186288e6-37e2-4512-9b7c-73b72b2d334a@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 09:32:37 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:

>On 18 Dec, 05:45, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Mon, 17 Dec 2007 14:28:07 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>probably still be using green screens too.

There were GUIs before Xerox. The ones most often mentioned were NLS by Engelbart at
Stanford, which had the first mouse, and Sketchpad by Sutherland at MIT. Both were in the
'60s. Xerox developed its Alto and Star during the '70s.

There were many GUI operating systems developed in the '80s. The most widely known were
Apple (II, Mac, Lisa) and OS/2. Others were PERQ, VisiOn, DESQView, GEM, GEOS, Amiga,
Atari. I wrote one. There were many more graphic applications such as Word, Excel, CAD,
desktop publishing. None of them were popular enough to change the MAINSTREAM, not even
Microsoft's Win3. Windows 95 did that.

ï¿½I had an enormous reservoir of goodwill towards Microsoft because it and it alone ï¿½
unlike Xerox, Apple, Amiga and many others who tried before it ï¿½ was the one that finally
delivered a usable graphical interface on ubiquitous, inexpensive hardware. Microsoft
often wasn't the first, and its software wasn't often the best, but it was inarguably the
one that delivered on the early promise of personal computing in a way no other software
maker did. Microsoft ï¿½ more than any other company ï¿½ opened up computing for ordinary
people. I loved Microsoft for that.ï¿½

ï¿½ Fred Langa, editor Byte magazine
```

```
On Dec 20, 2:34 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 09:32:37 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
> >On 18 Dec, 05:45, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Fred Langa, editor Byte magazine

Maybe, but it "delivered a usable graphical interface", not because
the users wanted it or chose it, but because MS contracted the box
makers with 'per box pricing', bundling of Windows 3.1 with MS-DOS (at
the same price as MS-DOS alone), and other methods found by the courts
to be illegal. It did this not so much to deliver a 'usable GUI' but
to drive DR-DOS, GEM, OS/2 and even BeOS out of the market by
strangling their 'air supply'.

At that time users generally did not want a GUI and Windows 3.x was
mainly used to run multiple DOS boxes on a 386.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lnsjm3lq48lifqk2su1puj2uimblstiror@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <186288e6-37e2-4512-9b7c-73b72b2d334a@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <vpejm35tpje1m4ivrg8m13i1jr4r14ivlu@4ax.com> <3ebc4c04-f364-4174-894f-07fa22a945fa@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 18:13:17 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Dec 20, 2:34 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
>> On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 09:32:37 -0800 (PST), Alistair <alist...@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
>strangling their 'air supply'.

Gates may have learned market domination from the history of National Cash Register under
John H. Patterson, founder and president 1884-1922. Patterson invented the concept of
'killer machine', created solely to destroy competition rather than make money. The
machines were sold below cost and sometimes even given away as free upgrades. After
competition was gone, he introduced the 'real' machine, priced for healthy profit. 

Thomas Watson Sr was a Patterson protoge. Wikipedia says "Patterson's methods influenced
United States business for a generation. In the period 1910-1930 it was estimated that
one-sixth of United States business executives were former NCR executives. .. Watson's
stint at NCR shaped him and helped him build the power of IBM. Both Patterson and Watson
were sentenced to one year imprisonment for unfair business practices, later overturned by
appeal. "

>At that time users generally did not want a GUI and Windows 3.x was
>mainly used to run multiple DOS boxes on a 386.

In its five year product life (1990-94), Windows 3.x 'sold' 10 million copies when the
number of PCs in the US was 50 million. That's only 20% penetration. In its three year
life (1995-97), Windows 95 sold 100 million copies, which was more than 90% penetration.
(The number of PCs in the US is now around 200 million.)

I didn't like and didn't use Windows 1-3. It reminded me of CICS. I LOVED Windows 95 when
it first came out, even though it killed MS-DOS, thereby obsoleting most of the
programming I had done for 14 years. It was evident to me that Win95 had a serious
dispatcher and memory manager in its kernel.  I can't believe how many people think, even
today, that Windows runs 'on top of DOS' a la Win3.
```

- **References:** `<fjpr2n$1c2$1@reader1.panix.com> <6969359c-00e5-4485-9e94-2e0e0acb1ee1@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <fjshnf$bjs$1@reader1.panix.com> <01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <186288e6-37e2-4512-9b7c-73b72b2d334a@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <vpejm35tpje1m4ivrg8m13i1jr4r14ivlu@4ax.com> <3ebc4c04-f364-4174-894f-07fa22a945fa@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <lnsjm3lq48lifqk2su1puj2uimblstiror@4ax.com>`

```
On Dec 20, 6:23 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 18:13:17 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Dec 20, 2:34 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
> (The number of PCs in the US is now around 200 million.)

That is because you almost couldn't buy a PC that didn't have Windows
95 on it. Previous you could buy one with MS-DOS or one with MS-DOS
+Windows, though MS made the latter the same price as DOS alone so
most had Windows.

The same with Vista. The sales figures are high not because people
_want_ Vista, generally they don't and install XP over the top, but
because MS has decreed that OEMs cannot sell a machine with XP.
Actually they have allowed this to happen.

> I didn't like and didn't use Windows 1-3. It reminded me of CICS. I LOVED Windows 95 when
> it first came out, even though it killed MS-DOS, thereby obsoleting most of the
> programming I had done for 14 years. It was evident to me that Win95 had a serious
> dispatcher and memory manager in its kernel.  I can't believe how many people think, even
> today, that Windows runs 'on top of DOS' a la Win3.

Duhhh. Windows 95 _does_ "run on top of DOS", as does 98 and ME. It is
only the NT series that doesn't.

In Windows 95 edit the MSDOS.SYS to change the loadgui=1 to =0 and it
will stop after MS-DOS 7 has loaded. Granted it doesn't rely so
heavily on the underlying DOS that is still there as 3.x did, but
win95 would not run if there was no MS-DOS under it.

You can load the W95 GUI with the WIN command, just like 3.x. Exiting
from this puts you back to the MS-DOS 7 prompt. It can be shown that
it is the _same_ MS-DOS and it was overwritten and reloaded on exit by
setting an environment variable before the 'win'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-12-21T00:23:20-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<atamm3dge9nvidhe5eriiodn6pm5qho0s1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<01k8m3p53kfh8f2j0ungcajdunflbka0as@4ax.com> <186288e6-37e2-4512-9b7c-73b72b2d334a@d4g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <vpejm35tpje1m4ivrg8m13i1jr4r14ivlu@4ax.com> <3ebc4c04-f364-4174-894f-07fa22a945fa@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <lnsjm3lq48lifqk2su1puj2uimblstiror@4ax.com> <55cb4140-86a4-4f27-ba41-1993a7bb03d5@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 22:14:24 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Dec 20, 6:23 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

>> >At that time users generally did not want a GUI and Windows 3.x was
>> >mainly used to run multiple DOS boxes on a 386.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>most had Windows.

I showed that only 20% of PCs had Windows in 1994.  During the period when Win3 was
available, 1990-94, I estimate there were 25 million PCs sold. About 8 million of them
came with Win3 (the other 2 million Win3s went to older machines). That's 32%, which is
less than most.

Between 1995 and 1998, the number of PCs went from 75 million to 120 million. Nearly all
the 45 milion new ones came with Win95, plus 55 million out of 75 (73%) old ones got it.
That's strong evidence people preferred Win95 over DOS. I think a big selling point was
they did not have to abandon their DOS applications. Win95 was 'a better DOS than MS-DOS,'
primarily due to better disk buffering. I worked for a company that sold a DOS disk cache
called Lightning. Sales declined between 1990 and 1994 (apparently Win3 didn't need it)
and plummeted to zero when Win95 came out.

>The same with Vista. The sales figures are high not because people
>_want_ Vista, generally they don't and install XP over the top, but
>because MS has decreed that OEMs cannot sell a machine with XP.
>Actually they have allowed this to happen.

Dell offers XP on its low-end Inspirion machines, not on its mid-range XPS series. I've
seen ads for Acer and Fujitsu with XP, although they may be old merchandise.
HP and Gateway deliver only Vista. 

>> I didn't like and didn't use Windows 1-3. It reminded me of CICS. I LOVED Windows 95 when
>> it first came out, even though it killed MS-DOS, thereby obsoleting most of the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>win95 would not run if there was no MS-DOS under it.

The Win 9x series used DOS 7 as a bootstrap loader, but did not use any DOS services while
running. DOS 7 was 16 bit real mode; Win 9x was 32 bit protected mode. In order to use the
underlying DOS, Windows would have to thunk the machine into 16 bit mode. The same applies
to the machine's BIOS, which Windows did not use either.

>You can load the W95 GUI with the WIN command, just like 3.x. Exiting
>from this puts you back to the MS-DOS 7 prompt. It can be shown that
>it is the _same_ MS-DOS and it was overwritten and reloaded on exit by
>setting an environment variable before the 'win'.

OK, DOS was there but wasn't used. DOS sessions under Win 9x and NT run as virtual
machines in virtual protected mode. Microsoft's 64 bit operating systems don't include
NTVDM, so cannot natively run DOS (and 16 bit Win3) programs. One must use an emulator to
run them. 

Uncontrolled programs are a security risk because they might be used to bypass DRM. Any
threat to Hollywood has to go.They've already plugged obscure leaks in VMWare; Intel
actually changed the instruction set to accomplish that. Now, if only they could stop
Linux hackers.
```

```
On Dec 21, 7:23 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
> On Wed, 19 Dec 2007 22:14:24 -0800 (PST), Richard <rip...@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> >On Dec 20, 6:23 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's strong evidence people preferred Win95 over DOS.

No. It was almost impossible to get a machine with MS-DOS on it.
Nearly all machines were preloaded with Win95, the buyer didn't have a
choice, or certainly not one easily found. It they wanted DOS they
installed it over the top on Win95.

As for the upgrades, most would have been Windows 3.x users so your
'preferred over DOS' is unfounded, it was preferred over Win3.x.

> I think a big selling point was
> they did not have to abandon their DOS applications. Win95 was 'a better DOS than MS-DOS,'

Actually it was OS/2 that was 'a better DOS'.

> primarily due to better disk buffering. I worked for a company that sold a DOS disk cache
> called Lightning. Sales declined between 1990 and 1994 (apparently Win3 didn't need it)
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> seen ads for Acer and Fujitsu with XP, although they may be old merchandise.

No, XP has been recently revived for those avoiding Vista.

> HP and Gateway deliver only Vista.
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> OK, DOS was there but wasn't used.

No. Not true. It wasn't used _much_ but Windows 95/98/ME did rely on
the PSP withing MS-DOS.

> DOS sessions under Win 9x and NT run as virtual
> machines in virtual protected mode. Microsoft's 64 bit operating systems don't include
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Linux hackers.

It is more likely that DRM will get blown away. Users don't want it,
many artists don't want it. If a hard disk fails your whole collection
vanishes and must be repurchased. Bugger that.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Robert <no@e.mail>
- **Date:** 2007-12-22T21:44:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgkrm39aogqvpeavb6qsbv5mfmafkrsgt9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <vpejm35tpje1m4ivrg8m13i1jr4r14ivlu@4ax.com> <3ebc4c04-f364-4174-894f-07fa22a945fa@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <lnsjm3lq48lifqk2su1puj2uimblstiror@4ax.com> <55cb4140-86a4-4f27-ba41-1993a7bb03d5@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <atamm3dge9nvidhe5eriiodn6pm5qho0s1@4ax.com> <0e41c350-c5ae-449f-9425-684f6a3879ec@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Thu, 20 Dec 2007 23:01:17 -0800 (PST), Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>On Dec 21, 7:23 pm, Robert <n...@e.mail> wrote:

>> The Win 9x series used DOS 7 as a bootstrap loader, but did not use any DOS services while
>> running. DOS 7 was 16 bit real mode; Win 9x was 32 bit protected mode. In order to use the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>the PSP within MS-DOS.

MS-DOS programs ignored the archaic Program Segment Prefix. What part of this PSP did Win
9x rely on?

Offset  Length  Description 
0       2       An INT 20h instruction is stored here
2       2       Program ending address
4       1       Unused, reserved by DOS
5       5       Call to DOS function dispatcher
0Ah     4       Address of program termination code
0Eh     4       Address of break handler routine
12h     4       Address of critical error handler routine
16h     22      Reserved for use by DOS
2Ch     2       Segment address of environment area
2Eh     34      Reserved by DOS
50h     3       INT 21h, RETF instructions
53h     9       Reserved by DOS
5Ch     16      Default FCB #1
6Ch     20      Default FCB #2
80h     1       Length of command line string
81h     127     Command line string

>> Uncontrolled programs are a security risk because they might be used to bypass DRM. Any
>> threat to Hollywood has to go.They've already plugged obscure leaks in VMWare; Intel
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>vanishes and must be repurchased. Bugger that.

DRM is a holdover from the concept of 'buying' a record or tape or software program.
Visionaries say the concept will  be replaced by pay-per-use. 

I'm not so sure about that. In the supermarket industry, produce can be sold by the pound
or piece. By the pound encourages customers to buy the least they can get by with. By the
piece encourages them to buy the biggest one in the bin. From a seller's point of view, by
the piece is better. 

Telephone companies have traditionally punished customers by charging per use (metered).
Enlightened ones offer unlimited use for a flat monthly fee. The latter collect more per
call AND have higher average usage. 

I see nothing wrong with 'buying' a record, but I do think we need to stop warez and
software piracy. If you have a solution less costly than DRM, the industry would love to
hear about it.
```

- **References:** `<c8lem3l2dqh1ih404pdl9m4pt5sglplolf@4ax.com> <4f40ae55-278d-4c01-b06b-3c9444ab50b8@i29g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <vpejm35tpje1m4ivrg8m13i1jr4r14ivlu@4ax.com> <3ebc4c04-f364-4174-894f-07fa22a945fa@e23g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <lnsjm3lq48lifqk2su1puj2uimblstiror@4ax.com> <55cb4140-86a4-4f27-ba41-1993a7bb03d5@s8g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <atamm3dge9nvidhe5eriiodn6pm5qho0s1@4ax.com> <0e41c350-c5ae-449f-9425-684f6a3879ec@s12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <cgkrm39aogqvpeavb6qsbv5mfmafkrsgt9@4ax.com> <b120358e-f752-4f8b-9871-1432684586be@x29g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <dasbj.30000$N67.3557@bignews5.bellsouth.net>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
>> DRM is trying to remove that freedom, and to remove rights that
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> what the manufacturer says in their literature.

Copyright must give way to a contract. If you agree to the producer's terms, 
no matter how egregious*, you are bound thereby. Converting the media from, 
say, CD to MP3, is even more problematic. I'd bet that if the producer 
agreed to replace your media (upon proof of purchase, etc.), then even 
copying for backup purposes would be disallowed.

--------
* There are exceptions: A contract cannot bind you to something illegal 
(overthrowing the government during the academic school year), against 
public policy (changing your religion), void on its face (involving a 
juvenile), or other limitations.
```

```
There's the argument that "the medium is (part of)the message": if a 
band requires me to play their CD while standing on my head and planting 
a flower in a place I normally wouldn't plant anything, that's a 
contract; not incredibly enforceable though.

But for argument's sake, why should we get to make backups ?  The 
purchase isn't IP, rather IP+media.

Part of the EULA, IIRC, states "contents of the box are substantially 
what's printed on the box"(ie: what's in the box is the part on the box 
that says "Box contents:")... since I don't see the word "crap" written 
anywhere on the box, they've already broken the contract.
```

```
"rpl" <plinnane3@yahoo.com.invalid> wrote:
> Alistair wrote:
>> "HeyBub" <hey...@gmail.com> wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> contract.

This is just in the U.S., of course, but the Supreme Court decision I
mentioned above was specifically addressing EULAs. The case was about
whether or not a user could make backup copies of the media if the EULA
forbid it. As I pointed out, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled unanimously
(*very* unlikely to be overridden) that the user has *the right* (not
privilege) to make up to two backup copies, regardless of any EULA. The
point (IIRC) was that keeping a user at risk of losing an application
that might be critical to their business, without the security of any
backup copies, was unreasonable, and struck down across the board.
```

```
"Alistair" <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote:
>"Judson McClendon" <ju...@sunvaley0.com> wrote:
>> This is just in the U.S., of course, but the Supreme Court decision I
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>A good decision by the Supreme Court. Will they extend it to film DVDs?

My understanding is that the principle hinges on the ability of the user
to have some reasonable security about their purchase, and that making
true backups in no way damages the vendor. I suspect the same principles
would apply to video as well, but I'm not sure what the courts have been
ruling. It's going to be pretty hard for a media vendor of any kind to
show that they're damaged when true backups are made and used only for
backup. I think it's notable that programs like DVD Shrink legally exist
to make backup copies of movie DVDs.

I don't think the courts have gone so far as to mandate that vendors make
their products 'copyable' in a physical sense.

Personally, though I'm meticulous about not making illegal copies of any
copyrighted product, I have no qualms about making myself a backup copy
of a movie. If I ever have a purchased movie that skips or hangs when
played, I make a backup copy. After all, I've already paid for it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: working storage values

```
Alistair wrote:
> On 30 Dec, 18:19, rpl <plinna...@yahoo.com.invalid> wrote:

>> But for argument's sake, why should we get to make backups ?  The
>> purchase isn't IP, rather IP+media.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> (rather than the 10 or so years that separates CDs, DVDs, HD-DVDs...)?

They don't even wait that long; my "old" DVD/CD-R/RW won't play most 
DVD's that are less than 5'ish years old.  Looks like they couldn't 
produce hardware with a MTBF of a couple years without having too many 
returns before the warranty ran out, so the DVD encryption keys scam 
compensates.

The only positive aspect is that the jackass who always wants to borrow 
your paid-for stuff so he can copy it ("come on man, just for a couple 
days, I'll give it right back, not like it's costing you anything...") 
is partially foiled... but so is my right to see the DVD I've purchased.

However I don't see how SCJ can make an across the board ruling that 
affects both entertainment and business software except as a stopgap 
measure.  Perhaps you should be able to buy "media insurance" to allow a 
backup to be made and kept around until they can send you replacement 
media.  Mind you in the case of business software, it *definitely* is 
the IP you're licensing (or should be).

rpl
```
